subtopic_detail: Post-menopausal osteoporosis - treatment initiation
stem: A 68-year-old post-menopausal woman is referred after a DXA scan. She has no history of fragility fractures. Her only medication is vitamin D 1000 IU daily and dietary calcium intake is adequate. She has no history of oesophageal disease, renal impairment, or malabsorption.

data_table.Investigations: DXA: T-score -2.8 lumbar spine, -2.1 femoral neck. Calcium 2.35 mmol/L (2.15-2.55). 25-OH vitamin D 68 nmol/L. eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73m2. TSH 2.1 mU/L (0.4-4.0).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological therapy?

options.text: Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every six months
options.rationale: Denosumab is effective but is reserved for patients who are intolerant of or have contraindications to oral bisphosphonates, or who have failed bisphosphonate therapy. It is not first-line in this uncomplicated presentation. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines)

options.text: Raloxifene 60 mg orally once daily with calcium
options.rationale: Raloxifene reduces vertebral fracture risk but has no proven benefit for hip fractures. It also carries VTE risk. Bisphosphonates have broader fracture reduction evidence and are preferred first-line. (Source: Osteoporosis Australia; eTG)

options.text: Alendronate 70 mg orally once weekly with calcium
options.rationale: Alendronate is the first-line pharmacological treatment for post-menopausal osteoporosis with T-score of -2.5 or below. It reduces vertebral, hip, and non-vertebral fractures. She has no contraindications to oral bisphosphonates. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines; eTG)

options.text: Zoledronic acid 5 mg intravenously once yearly
options.rationale: IV zoledronic acid is an alternative bisphosphonate typically reserved for patients who cannot tolerate oral formulations, have poor adherence, or have upper GI contraindications. Oral bisphosphonate is trialled first. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines)

options.text: Teriparatide 20 mcg subcutaneously once daily
options.rationale: Teriparatide is an anabolic agent reserved for severe osteoporosis with multiple fractures or failure of anti-resorptive therapy. PBS-subsidised criteria are strict and this patient does not meet them. (Source: PBS Schedule; AMH)

explanation.summary: Post-menopausal osteoporosis is defined by a T-score of -2.5 or below at the lumbar spine or femoral neck on DXA, or by a minimal trauma fracture regardless of T-score. First-line pharmacological treatment is an oral bisphosphonate, with alendronate being the most commonly prescribed. Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake are ensured concurrently. Denosumab, IV bisphosphonates, and anabolic agents are reserved for specific indications including bisphosphonate intolerance or severe disease.
key_points: T-score of -2.5 or below at spine or hip establishes osteoporosis diagnosis
Oral bisphosphonates are first-line; denosumab is second-line
Alendronate must be taken upright with water 30 minutes before food
PBS criteria for teriparatide require severe osteoporosis with fractures
explanation.pearls: Alendronate reduces hip fracture risk by approximately 40% over 3-4 years in women with established osteoporosis, with efficacy maintained for at least 10 years of continuous therapy per the FLEX extension trial.

subtopic_detail: Lewy body dementia - recognition and initial management
stem: A 74-year-old man is brought by his wife who reports fluctuating alertness over three months, with episodes of being lucid one day and confused the next. He has vivid visual hallucinations of children in the garden that he describes in detail. His wife also notes he has become slower in his movements with a shuffling gait. He has no psychiatric history. A CT brain shows mild generalised cortical atrophy without focal lesions.

data_table.Examination: Alert but mildly confused. MMSE 21/30. Bilateral upper limb cogwheel rigidity. Mild resting tremor left hand. Shuffling gait with reduced arm swing.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs, B12 - all normal. CT brain: mild generalised atrophy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?

options.text: Donepezil with gradual titration and regular review
options.rationale: While donepezil is a cholinesterase inhibitor used in Lewy body dementia, rivastigmine has the strongest evidence base specifically for DLB and is TGA-approved for Parkinson's disease dementia, a closely related condition. (Source: eTG Neurology; NHMRC Dementia Guidelines)

options.text: Rivastigmine with cautious dose titration and review
options.rationale: Rivastigmine is the cholinesterase inhibitor with the strongest evidence in DLB and is TGA-approved for related Parkinson's disease dementia. It improves cognition, hallucinations, and behavioural symptoms. Antipsychotics must be avoided due to severe neuroleptic sensitivity. (Source: eTG Neurology; AMH)

options.text: Haloperidol low dose for hallucination management
options.rationale: Haloperidol is absolutely contraindicated in DLB due to severe neuroleptic sensitivity, which causes marked worsening of parkinsonism, rigidity, sedation, and can be fatal. This is a critical safety point in DLB management. (Source: eTG Neurology; AMH)

options.text: Levodopa-carbidopa for motor symptom improvement
options.rationale: While levodopa can be cautiously used for motor symptoms in DLB, it is not the initial priority when cognitive and neuropsychiatric symptoms predominate. Additionally, levodopa can worsen hallucinations. Cholinesterase inhibitors address both cognitive and psychiatric domains. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Memantine for moderate stage cognitive stabilisation
options.rationale: Memantine has limited evidence in DLB compared to cholinesterase inhibitors and is typically reserved for moderate-to-severe stages or as adjunctive therapy. Cholinesterase inhibitors are the initial pharmacological choice. (Source: NHMRC Dementia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Dementia with Lewy bodies is characterised by the triad of fluctuating cognition, recurrent visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism. A fourth core feature is REM sleep behaviour disorder. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly rivastigmine, are the mainstay of pharmacological treatment. The most critical management principle is absolute avoidance of typical antipsychotics and cautious use of atypicals, as patients have severe neuroleptic sensitivity that can be fatal.
key_points: Core features: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour disorder
Rivastigmine is the cholinesterase inhibitor with strongest DLB evidence
Antipsychotic sensitivity is a defining diagnostic feature of DLB
Haloperidol is contraindicated and potentially fatal in DLB
explanation.pearls: Neuroleptic sensitivity in DLB affects approximately 50% of patients exposed to antipsychotics, with typical agents causing irreversible deterioration in 30-50% of cases, often progressing to fatal rigidity and autonomic failure within days.

subtopic_detail: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy - assessment and management
stem: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with progressive numbness and burning pain in both feet over 6 months. He describes the pain as worse at night and rates it 7/10. He has been on metformin 2 g daily and gliclazide 160 mg daily. His most recent HbA1c was 8.2% (66 mmol/mol).

data_table.Examination: Reduced sensation to monofilament and vibration bilaterally below mid-calf. Ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Normal power in all muscle groups. Foot pulses palpable.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.2% (66 mmol/mol). B12 280 pmol/L (>150). TFTs normal. eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m2.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological agent for his neuropathic pain?

options.text: Oxycodone 5 mg twice daily with regular review
options.rationale: Opioids are not recommended for diabetic neuropathic pain due to limited evidence of long-term benefit, risk of dependence, and inferior efficacy compared to first-line agents. Australian guidelines explicitly advise against opioid use in this setting. (Source: eTG Pain; Diabetes Australia)

options.text: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with meals
options.rationale: NSAIDs are ineffective for neuropathic pain as the mechanism is nerve damage rather than peripheral inflammation. This patient also has early CKD (eGFR 68) making NSAIDs relatively contraindicated. (Source: eTG Pain)

options.text: Paracetamol 1 g four times daily with reassessment
options.rationale: Paracetamol has no demonstrated efficacy in neuropathic pain. It may have a role as adjunctive therapy for mixed nociceptive-neuropathic pain but is not appropriate as monotherapy here. (Source: eTG Pain)

options.text: Amitriptyline 10 mg at night with gradual titration
options.rationale: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants such as amitriptyline are first-line for painful diabetic neuropathy per Australian guidelines, alongside pregabalin and duloxetine. Amitriptyline is started at 10 mg nightly and titrated to effect or a maximum of 75 mg. (Source: eTG Pain; AMH; Diabetes Australia)

options.text: Carbamazepine 100 mg twice daily with dose titration
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia but is not recommended for diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Its evidence is limited to trigeminal and glossopharyngeal neuralgias. First-line options for diabetic neuropathy are amitriptyline, pregabalin, or duloxetine. (Source: eTG Pain; AMH)

explanation.summary: Painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy affects up to 25% of patients with diabetes and typically presents as a symmetric distal sensorimotor neuropathy with burning, tingling, or shooting pain. First-line pharmacotherapy includes tricyclic antidepressants, pregabalin, or duloxetine. Glycaemic optimisation is essential to slow progression. NSAIDs and opioids have no recommended role in neuropathic pain management.
key_points: First-line agents: amitriptyline, pregabalin, or duloxetine
Optimise glycaemic control to slow neuropathy progression
Annual monofilament screening detects loss of protective sensation
NSAIDs and opioids are ineffective or inappropriate for neuropathic pain
explanation.pearls: Amitriptyline at analgesic doses (10-75 mg nocte) provides number needed to treat of approximately 3.6 for painful diabetic neuropathy, with onset of benefit typically within 2-4 weeks of reaching therapeutic dose.

subtopic_detail: Bronchiectasis exacerbation - antibiotic selection with Pseudomonas
stem: A 57-year-old woman with known bronchiectasis presents with a 5-day history of increased sputum volume and purulence. She is febrile at 38.2 degrees Celsius with an oxygen saturation of 95% on room air. Her previous sputum cultures have grown Pseudomonas aeruginosa on two occasions in the past year. She has no penicillin allergy and her renal function is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92 bpm, BP 128/76 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air, Temp 38.2 degrees C.
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, neutrophils 11.8 x10^9/L. CRP 68 mg/L. Sputum sent for MC&S.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric oral antibiotic?

options.text: Ciprofloxacin 750 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: In bronchiectasis with previous Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolation, ciprofloxacin is the recommended first-line oral agent as it provides reliable anti-pseudomonal activity. A 14-day course is standard for exacerbations. The dose is 750 mg twice daily, higher than the standard 500 mg for other indications. (Source: eTG Respiratory; TSANZ)

options.text: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is appropriate for bronchiectasis exacerbations when Haemophilus influenzae or Streptococcus pneumoniae is the predominant pathogen, but it has no activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Given her previous Pseudomonas isolates, an anti-pseudomonal agent is required. (Source: eTG Respiratory)

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Doxycycline is an alternative for non-pseudomonal bronchiectasis exacerbations but has no activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It would be inappropriate given her recurrent Pseudomonas isolation. (Source: eTG Respiratory)

options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate broadens cover to include beta-lactamase producing organisms but still lacks Pseudomonas activity. It is not appropriate when previous cultures have repeatedly grown Pseudomonas aeruginosa. (Source: eTG Respiratory)

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg orally once daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Azithromycin has a role in long-term suppressive therapy for bronchiectasis to reduce exacerbation frequency but has no reliable Pseudomonas activity. It is not appropriate as acute exacerbation therapy in Pseudomonas-colonised patients. (Source: eTG Respiratory; TSANZ)

explanation.summary: Bronchiectasis exacerbations are treated with antibiotics guided by previous sputum microbiology. When Pseudomonas aeruginosa has been previously isolated, oral ciprofloxacin is the mainstay of empiric therapy due to its reliable oral bioavailability and anti-pseudomonal activity. Courses of 14 days are standard. Sputum should be sent before starting antibiotics to guide tailored therapy. Long-term azithromycin may be considered for patients with three or more exacerbations per year.
key_points: Previous sputum microbiology guides empiric antibiotic choice in bronchiectasis
Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BD is first-line oral agent for Pseudomonas
Standard treatment duration is 14 days for bronchiectasis exacerbations
Azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly reduces exacerbation frequency in recurrent disease
explanation.pearls: Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BD (not the standard 500 mg BD used for UTI) is required for Pseudomonas in bronchiectasis to achieve adequate bronchial tissue concentrations, reflecting the higher MIC of Pseudomonas compared to Enterobacteriaceae.

subtopic_detail: Pulmonary sarcoidosis - corticosteroid initiation decision
stem: A 36-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of dry cough, bilateral ankle pain, and tender erythematous nodules on her shins. She has recently noticed blurred vision in her left eye. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Serum ACE is elevated at 95 U/L (8-52). A bronchoscopy with biopsy reveals non-caseating granulomata.

data_table.Investigations: CXR: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with upper lobe infiltrates. Serum ACE 95 U/L (8-52). Calcium 2.62 mmol/L (2.15-2.55). FBC, UEC, LFTs normal. Slit lamp examination: anterior uveitis left eye.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Observation with repeat chest X-ray in three months
options.rationale: Observation is appropriate for stage I sarcoidosis (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy alone) without extrapulmonary complications. This patient has stage II disease with parenchymal infiltrates, active uveitis, and hypercalcaemia, all of which are indications for systemic corticosteroids. (Source: eTG Respiratory; TSANZ)

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily as sole therapy
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine has a role in sarcoidosis-related skin disease and arthralgia but is not adequate for pulmonary infiltrates, uveitis, or hypercalcaemia. Systemic corticosteroids are required for organ-threatening disease. (Source: eTG Respiratory)

options.text: Methotrexate 10 mg weekly as steroid-sparing first-line
options.rationale: Methotrexate is used as a steroid-sparing agent in sarcoidosis but is not appropriate as initial monotherapy. It is introduced when corticosteroid dose cannot be tapered below an acceptable threshold or as first-line only when corticosteroids are contraindicated. (Source: eTG Respiratory)

options.text: Inhaled budesonide with topical eye drops only
options.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids are insufficient for stage II pulmonary sarcoidosis with systemic involvement. Topical eye drops treat anterior uveitis but systemic corticosteroids are needed for the pulmonary infiltrates and hypercalcaemia, which carry risk of renal damage. (Source: eTG Respiratory; TSANZ)

options.text: Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg daily with ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Systemic prednisolone is indicated for stage II sarcoidosis with pulmonary infiltrates, active uveitis, and hypercalcaemia. Starting dose is typically 0.5 mg/kg/day (approximately 30-40 mg daily) tapered over 6-12 months. Concurrent ophthalmology review is essential for uveitis management. (Source: eTG Respiratory; TSANZ)

explanation.summary: Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease diagnosed by non-caseating granulomata with compatible clinical and radiological features after excluding other causes. Systemic corticosteroids are indicated for symptomatic stage II-IV pulmonary disease, hypercalcaemia, cardiac or neurological involvement, and sight-threatening uveitis. Stage I disease without extrapulmonary complications may be observed as up to 70% resolve spontaneously. Steroid-sparing agents such as methotrexate or azathioprine are introduced when prolonged high-dose steroids are required.
key_points: Stage I (BHL alone) often self-resolves and can be observed
Indications for systemic steroids: pulmonary infiltrates, uveitis, hypercalcaemia, cardiac or neurological involvement
Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day is standard initial dose with slow taper over 6-12 months
Serum ACE level supports diagnosis but is not specific and should not guide treatment decisions alone
explanation.pearls: Sarcoidosis-associated hypercalcaemia results from unregulated 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity in granulomata converting 25-OH vitamin D to calcitriol, meaning exogenous vitamin D supplementation can paradoxically worsen hypercalcaemia in active sarcoidosis.

subtopic_detail: Obstructive sleep apnoea - diagnosis and treatment initiation
stem: A 52-year-old man is referred by his GP for excessive daytime somnolence. His wife reports loud snoring with witnessed apnoeic episodes most nights. He has a BMI of 36 kg/m2, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 14 out of 24. An in-laboratory polysomnography shows an apnoea-hypopnoea index of 32 events per hour.

data_table.Investigations: Polysomnography: AHI 32/hr, lowest SpO2 78%, arousal index 44/hr. HbA1c 7.8%. Haemoglobin 152 g/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Weight loss counselling and positional therapy only
options.rationale: While weight loss is an important adjunctive measure, it is not sufficient as sole therapy for severe OSA (AHI 32). Positional therapy alone is appropriate only when supine-predominant mild OSA is demonstrated. CPAP is the first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA. (Source: TSANZ; Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: Mandibular advancement splint with dental review
options.rationale: Mandibular advancement devices are appropriate for mild-to-moderate OSA or for patients who cannot tolerate CPAP. For severe OSA (AHI >=30) with significant desaturation, CPAP is the recommended first-line therapy due to superior efficacy. (Source: TSANZ; Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty referral for assessment
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is not first-line for OSA and has variable efficacy, particularly for severe disease. It may be considered for anatomical obstruction not responsive to CPAP, but conservative management with CPAP should be trialled first. (Source: TSANZ)

options.text: Nocturnal supplemental oxygen via nasal cannulae
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen alone does not treat the obstructive events in OSA and may prolong apnoeas by blunting the hypoxic arousal response. CPAP prevents airway collapse, which is the underlying pathology. Supplemental oxygen has a role only in overlap syndrome with COPD. (Source: TSANZ)

options.text: Continuous positive airway pressure titration trial
options.rationale: CPAP is the first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA (AHI >=15), particularly with symptomatic daytime somnolence and significant desaturation. An AHI of 32 is severe OSA. CPAP eliminates apnoeas, reduces cardiovascular risk, and improves daytime function. A titration study determines the optimal pressure. (Source: TSANZ; Australasian Sleep Association)

explanation.summary: Obstructive sleep apnoea is classified by AHI as mild (5-14), moderate (15-29), or severe (>=30 events per hour). CPAP is the gold standard treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA, reducing apnoeas, improving oxygenation, and decreasing cardiovascular risk. Weight loss is an important adjunct, with 10% weight loss potentially halving the AHI. Mandibular advancement devices are alternatives for mild-moderate disease or CPAP intolerance. An Epworth Sleepiness Scale >=10 indicates significant subjective sleepiness warranting investigation.
key_points: Severe OSA is defined as AHI >=30 events per hour
CPAP is first-line for moderate-to-severe OSA
Mandibular advancement devices are alternatives for mild-to-moderate disease
Weight loss of 10% can reduce AHI by approximately 50%
explanation.pearls: Untreated severe OSA (AHI >=30) is associated with a 3-fold increase in cardiovascular mortality and a 6-fold increase in motor vehicle accident risk, with CPAP adherence of >=4 hours per night needed to achieve meaningful cardiovascular benefit.

subtopic_detail: Melanoma - recognition and excision biopsy
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to your clinic concerned about a mole on his upper back that has been changing over 4 months. On examination, the lesion is an asymmetric, irregularly bordered, variably pigmented macule measuring 9 mm in diameter. There is a darkened area at the 2 o'clock position. Dermoscopy reveals an atypical pigment network, blue-white veil, and irregular dots and globules. He has a family history of melanoma in his father.

data_table.Examination: Upper back: 9 mm asymmetric, irregularly bordered macule. Variable pigmentation (brown, dark brown, black areas). Dermatoscopy: atypical pigment network, blue-white veil, irregular dots and globules. No palpable regional lymphadenopathy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Excision biopsy with 2 mm clinical margins
options.rationale: A clinically suspicious pigmented lesion with dermoscopic features of melanoma requires excision biopsy with narrow (2 mm) clinical margins for histopathological diagnosis. This allows complete assessment of Breslow thickness, which determines definitive wide local excision margins. Punch, shave, and incisional biopsies are inadequate for suspected melanoma. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Melanoma CPG; RACGP)

options.text: Punch biopsy from the most suspicious area
options.rationale: Punch biopsy samples only a portion of the lesion and may miss the area of greatest Breslow thickness, leading to understaging. Excision biopsy is the recommended diagnostic method for suspected melanoma to allow full histopathological assessment. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Melanoma CPG)

options.text: Shave biopsy to determine lesion histology
options.rationale: Shave biopsy transects the deep margin and prevents accurate measurement of Breslow thickness, which is the most important prognostic factor in melanoma. It is contraindicated for lesions suspicious for melanoma. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Melanoma CPG)

options.text: Wide local excision with 10 mm clinical margins
options.rationale: Wide local excision is the definitive surgical treatment after melanoma diagnosis, with margins determined by Breslow thickness. Performing wide excision without a prior diagnostic biopsy is inappropriate as it commits to potentially unnecessary tissue removal and may complicate sentinel lymph node biopsy. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Melanoma CPG)

options.text: Photographic monitoring with review in 3 months
options.rationale: Sequential digital dermoscopic imaging is appropriate for monitoring low-risk lesions that do not meet clinical or dermoscopic criteria for melanoma. This lesion has multiple high-risk features (ABCDE criteria, blue-white veil on dermoscopy, family history) requiring immediate excision biopsy. Delay risks progression. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Melanoma CPG; RACGP)

explanation.summary: Suspected melanoma should be managed with excision biopsy using narrow (2 mm) clinical margins to allow full histopathological assessment including Breslow thickness measurement. Breslow thickness then determines the definitive wide local excision margins: less than 1 mm requires 1 cm margins; 1-2 mm requires 1-2 cm; greater than 2 mm requires 2 cm margins. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is discussed for lesions with Breslow thickness greater than 1 mm. Punch, shave, and incisional biopsies are discouraged as they may result in inaccurate staging.
key_points: Excision biopsy with 2 mm margins is the diagnostic standard for suspected melanoma
Breslow thickness is the most important prognostic factor
WLE margins: less than 1 mm Breslow = 1 cm; 1-2 mm = 1-2 cm; greater than 2 mm = 2 cm
SLNB is discussed for Breslow greater than 1 mm
explanation.pearls: The blue-white veil on dermoscopy corresponds to an acanthotic epidermis overlying dermal melanin and correlates with invasive melanoma in 85% of cases, making it one of the most specific dermoscopic features for malignancy.

subtopic_detail: Colorectal cancer screening - investigation of iron deficiency anaemia
stem: A 63-year-old post-menopausal woman presents with a 3-month history of increasing fatigue. She has no abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or change in bowel habit. She has no family history of colorectal cancer. She has not participated in the National Bowel Cancer Screening Program despite receiving her kit.

data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L (120-150). MCV 72 fL (80-100). Ferritin 8 mcg/L (20-200). Iron 4 micromol/L (10-30). Transferrin saturation 7% (15-45). Coeliac serology: tTG-IgA negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Faecal immunochemical test and dietary iron review
options.rationale: While iFOBT is the population screening tool, this patient already has confirmed iron deficiency anaemia. A negative iFOBT does not exclude colorectal malignancy, and direct endoscopic investigation is required. iFOBT is for asymptomatic screening, not for investigating established IDA. (Source: GESA; Cancer Council Australia)

options.text: Oral iron replacement with repeat bloods in 8 weeks
options.rationale: While iron replacement is needed, investigating the cause of iron deficiency anaemia must occur concurrently. Treating the anaemia without identifying the underlying cause risks missing a colorectal malignancy, which is the most important diagnosis to exclude in this age group. (Source: GESA; eTG Gastro)

options.text: Bidirectional endoscopy with gastroscopy and colonoscopy
options.rationale: Post-menopausal women and all men with unexplained iron deficiency anaemia require both gastroscopy and colonoscopy to exclude upper and lower GI malignancy and other causes of occult blood loss. Both procedures are recommended simultaneously as both sites contribute significantly. (Source: GESA IDA Guidelines; eTG Gastro; BSG)

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with oral and IV contrast
options.rationale: CT abdomen and pelvis may detect advanced colorectal cancers but lacks the sensitivity for early mucosal lesions and polyps. It also cannot provide tissue sampling. Endoscopy is the gold standard for investigating IDA as it allows direct visualisation and biopsy. (Source: GESA; Cancer Council Australia)

options.text: Colonoscopy alone with faecal calprotectin testing
options.rationale: Colonoscopy alone misses upper GI pathology, which accounts for approximately 30% of causes of IDA. Bidirectional endoscopy is recommended. Faecal calprotectin is used for IBD assessment and does not replace the need for upper GI investigation. (Source: GESA IDA Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Unexplained iron deficiency anaemia in post-menopausal women and all men warrants bidirectional endoscopy to exclude gastrointestinal malignancy. Colorectal cancer is the most common GI malignancy causing IDA, but upper GI pathology including gastric cancer and coeliac disease also contribute. The National Bowel Cancer Screening Program recommends biennial iFOBT for asymptomatic adults aged 50-74, but once IDA is established, endoscopic investigation is mandatory. Coeliac serology should be checked concurrently, and iron replacement should begin while investigations are arranged.
key_points: Post-menopausal women and all men with IDA require bidirectional endoscopy
Both gastroscopy and colonoscopy are done simultaneously to assess both sites
iFOBT is a screening tool only and does not replace endoscopy for investigating IDA
NBCSP recommends biennial iFOBT for 50-74 year olds
explanation.pearls: Approximately 10-15% of patients with IDA have synchronous upper and lower GI pathology, which is why bidirectional endoscopy rather than colonoscopy alone is recommended as the initial investigation by GESA guidelines.

subtopic_detail: Breast lump - triple assessment approach
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a firm, non-tender, 2 cm lump in the upper outer quadrant of her right breast that she noticed 3 weeks ago. It is not tethered to skin but has limited mobility. There is no nipple discharge, skin dimpling, or axillary lymphadenopathy. She has no family history of breast or ovarian cancer. Her last mammogram through BreastScreen was 3 years ago and was normal.

data_table.Examination: Right breast: 2 cm firm, non-tender mass at 2 o'clock position. Not fixed to skin or chest wall, limited mobility. No nipple retraction or discharge. No skin changes. No axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in investigation?

options.text: Repeat clinical examination in 6 weeks for reassessment
options.rationale: A new palpable breast lump in a 45-year-old woman requires prompt investigation. Watchful waiting risks delaying diagnosis of breast cancer, which is the most common cancer in Australian women. Triple assessment should be initiated at presentation. (Source: Cancer Australia; RACGP)

options.text: Mammography and targeted ultrasound with core biopsy
options.rationale: Triple assessment - clinical examination, imaging (mammography plus targeted ultrasound), and tissue sampling (core needle biopsy) - is the standard of care for investigating a palpable breast lump. In women aged 35 and over, mammography is the initial imaging modality, supplemented by targeted ultrasound. Core biopsy provides histological diagnosis. (Source: Cancer Australia; RACGP; BreastScreen Australia)

options.text: Breast ultrasound alone with fine needle aspiration
options.rationale: While ultrasound is useful, mammography is also required in women aged 35 and over as it provides complementary information including calcification patterns. Fine needle aspiration provides cytology but not histology and has been largely replaced by core biopsy, which provides architectural information needed for diagnosis and receptor status. (Source: Cancer Australia)

options.text: Breast MRI to characterise the lesion before biopsy
options.rationale: Breast MRI is not a standard first-line investigation for a palpable breast lump. It is reserved for specific indications including high-risk screening (BRCA carriers), assessing multifocal disease, or evaluating the contralateral breast. Standard triple assessment with mammography, ultrasound, and core biopsy is the initial pathway. (Source: Cancer Australia; RACGP)

options.text: Excisional biopsy under general anaesthesia for diagnosis
options.rationale: Excisional biopsy is no longer recommended as a diagnostic procedure. Image-guided core needle biopsy provides tissue diagnosis with minimal morbidity. Excisional biopsy may be performed when core biopsy is discordant with imaging or if core biopsy is technically not feasible. (Source: Cancer Australia)

explanation.summary: The triple assessment approach for breast lumps combines clinical examination, imaging, and tissue sampling to achieve diagnostic accuracy exceeding 99%. For women aged 35 and over, imaging consists of mammography plus targeted ultrasound. Core needle biopsy has replaced fine needle aspiration as the tissue sampling method of choice because it provides histological architecture and receptor status. BreastScreen Australia provides biennial screening mammography for women aged 50-74, with the program available to women 40-49 on request.
key_points: Triple assessment: clinical exam, imaging (mammography + ultrasound), core biopsy
Mammography is the initial imaging modality for women aged 35 and over
Core biopsy provides histology and receptor status, superior to FNA
BreastScreen recommends biennial mammography for 50-74 year olds
explanation.pearls: Core needle biopsy uses a 14-gauge needle under ultrasound guidance and obtains tissue cores that allow assessment of tumour grade, receptor status (ER, PR, HER2), and Ki-67 index, enabling treatment planning before definitive surgery.

subtopic_detail: Spirometry interpretation - obstructive vs restrictive
stem: A 64-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea over 2 years. He has a chronic productive cough. He has never had spirometry performed. He is currently taking no regular respiratory medications.

data_table.Investigations: Spirometry (post-bronchodilator): FEV1 1.42 L (predicted 3.20 L, 44% predicted). FVC 3.10 L (predicted 4.15 L, 75% predicted). FEV1/FVC ratio 0.46. Bronchodilator response: FEV1 increase 80 mL (5.6%).

lead_in: Which spirometric pattern and severity classification is correct?

options.text: Moderate obstructive defect with significant reversibility
options.rationale: The FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.46 confirms obstruction. However, FEV1 of 44% predicted classifies this as severe, not moderate (GOLD grade 3: 30-49%). The bronchodilator response of 80 mL (5.6%) does not meet the threshold for significant reversibility (>=200 mL AND >=12%). (Source: TSANZ; GOLD 2024)

options.text: Restrictive defect with reduced total lung capacity
options.rationale: The FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.46 is below the lower limit of normal, confirming an obstructive pattern. A restrictive defect would show a normal or elevated FEV1/FVC ratio with reduced FVC and requires confirmation by plethysmography showing reduced total lung capacity. (Source: TSANZ)

options.text: Mixed obstructive and restrictive defect pattern
options.rationale: While FVC is mildly reduced at 75% predicted, this is a common finding in severe airflow obstruction due to gas trapping, not restriction. A mixed pattern requires confirmed reduced TLC on plethysmography in addition to the low FEV1/FVC ratio. The clinical picture is consistent with pure airflow obstruction. (Source: TSANZ)

options.text: Severe obstructive defect with no significant reversibility
options.rationale: FEV1/FVC ratio 0.46 confirms obstruction. Post-bronchodilator FEV1 of 44% predicted classifies this as GOLD grade 3 (severe: 30-49%). Bronchodilator response of 80 mL (5.6%) does not meet the significance threshold of >=200 mL AND >=12% increase. This is consistent with severe COPD. (Source: TSANZ; GOLD 2024)

options.text: Mild obstructive defect consistent with early disease
options.rationale: FEV1 of 44% predicted is far below the threshold for mild obstruction. GOLD classifies mild as FEV1 >=80% predicted (grade 1). This patient has severe airflow limitation with significant impact on exercise capacity. (Source: GOLD 2024)

explanation.summary: Spirometry is the gold standard for diagnosing and classifying airflow obstruction. A post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio below 0.70 (or below the lower limit of normal) confirms obstruction. GOLD classifies severity by post-bronchodilator FEV1 as percentage of predicted: grade 1 mild (>=80%), grade 2 moderate (50-79%), grade 3 severe (30-49%), grade 4 very severe (<30%). Significant bronchodilator reversibility requires both an increase of >=200 mL AND >=12% from baseline. Reduced FVC in the setting of obstruction usually reflects gas trapping rather than true restriction.
key_points: Obstruction is confirmed by post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC below 0.70
GOLD severity: grade 1 >=80%, grade 2 50-79%, grade 3 30-49%, grade 4 <30%
Significant reversibility: >=200 mL AND >=12% increase in FEV1
Reduced FVC with low ratio usually reflects gas trapping, not restriction
explanation.pearls: Post-bronchodilator values are used for COPD classification because pre-bronchodilator testing overestimates obstruction severity, while the fixed 0.70 ratio threshold overdiagnoses COPD in patients over 70 where the lower limit of normal may be as low as 0.65.

subtopic_detail: Plaque psoriasis - treatment escalation
stem: A 42-year-old man presents with chronic plaque psoriasis affecting approximately 12% of his body surface area, involving his elbows, knees, trunk, and scalp. He has been using betamethasone dipropionate ointment and calcipotriol cream for 8 months with diminishing response. He is otherwise healthy with no hepatitis B or C risk factors. His psoriasis significantly impacts his quality of life.

data_table.Investigations: FBC normal. LFTs normal. Hepatitis B surface antigen negative. Hepatitis C antibody negative. eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73m2.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in treatment?

options.text: Narrowband UVB phototherapy three times weekly
options.rationale: For moderate-to-severe psoriasis (BSA >10%) inadequately controlled by topical therapy, narrowband UVB phototherapy is the recommended next step before systemic agents. It is effective, well-tolerated, and avoids systemic immunosuppression. Three times weekly is the standard frequency. (Source: ACD Psoriasis Guidelines; eTG Dermatology)

options.text: Higher potency clobetasol propionate ointment
options.rationale: Clobetasol is a superpotent topical corticosteroid that carries significant side effect risk with prolonged use including skin atrophy and HPA axis suppression. Escalating topical potency is not appropriate when 12% BSA is involved and current topical therapy has failed. Systemic or phototherapy options are more appropriate. (Source: eTG Dermatology; ACD)

options.text: Oral prednisolone taper over four to six weeks
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids are generally avoided in psoriasis due to risk of rebound flare on withdrawal, which can be severe and include transformation to pustular or erythrodermic psoriasis. They are not part of the standard treatment ladder. (Source: ACD Psoriasis Guidelines)

options.text: Adalimumab subcutaneous injection fortnightly
options.rationale: Biologic agents such as adalimumab are highly effective for severe psoriasis but are reserved for patients who have failed conventional systemic therapy (methotrexate or ciclosporin) and phototherapy. PBS criteria require documentation of inadequate response to these agents before biologic access. (Source: PBS Schedule; ACD)

options.text: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly with folate supplement
options.rationale: Methotrexate is appropriate for moderate-to-severe psoriasis but is typically introduced after phototherapy has been trialled or when phototherapy is impractical. In the treatment ladder, phototherapy precedes systemic agents for most patients. Methotrexate would be appropriate if phototherapy is inaccessible or insufficient. (Source: ACD Psoriasis Guidelines; eTG Dermatology)

explanation.summary: The psoriasis treatment ladder progresses from topical agents for mild disease (BSA <5%), to phototherapy for moderate disease, to conventional systemic agents (methotrexate, ciclosporin) for severe or refractory disease, and finally to biologic therapies. Narrowband UVB phototherapy is effective for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis and is the standard next step after topical treatment failure. Systemic corticosteroids are avoided due to rebound risk. Biologic access through the PBS requires documentation of failure on conventional agents.
key_points: Treatment ladder: topicals, phototherapy, systemic DMARDs, biologics
NB-UVB phototherapy is first step after topical failure for moderate disease
Systemic corticosteroids risk severe rebound including pustular transformation
PBS biologic access requires failed conventional systemic therapy
explanation.pearls: Narrowband UVB phototherapy achieves PASI 75 response in approximately 70% of patients with moderate plaque psoriasis within 20-30 treatments, with response often sustained for 3-6 months after completing a course.

subtopic_detail: Ankylosing spondylitis - diagnosis and initial NSAID therapy
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 12-month history of progressive low back pain and stiffness that is worst in the morning, lasting 90 minutes, and improves with activity. He wakes at night with pain and finds that exercise helps. He has a history of anterior uveitis in his left eye 6 months ago. His father has ankylosing spondylitis. NSAIDs have not been trialled.

data_table.Examination: Reduced lumbar flexion. Positive modified Schober test (expansion <4 cm). Reduced chest expansion (2.5 cm). Bilateral sacroiliac joint tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: CRP 28 mg/L (<5). ESR 32 mm/hr (<20). HLA-B27 positive. Pelvic X-ray: bilateral sacroiliitis grade 2.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological therapy?

options.text: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly with folate supplementation
options.rationale: Conventional DMARDs such as methotrexate have no proven efficacy for axial disease in ankylosing spondylitis. They may have a limited role for peripheral arthritis but are not part of the axial treatment pathway. NSAIDs are the established first-line treatment. (Source: ARA; EULAR/ASAS Recommendations)

options.text: Adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously fortnightly
options.rationale: TNF inhibitors are highly effective for axial spondyloarthritis but are second-line therapy. Australian guidelines and PBS criteria require documented failure of at least two NSAIDs at full dose over a minimum of 4 weeks each before biologic access. (Source: ARA; PBS Schedule)

options.text: Sulfasalazine 1 g twice daily with dose titration
options.rationale: Sulfasalazine has a role in peripheral arthritis associated with spondyloarthritis but is ineffective for axial symptoms including spinal pain and stiffness. NSAIDs are the correct first-line therapy for axial disease. (Source: ARA; EULAR/ASAS Recommendations)

options.text: Prednisolone 25 mg daily with gastric protection
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids are not recommended for axial spondyloarthritis as they have limited efficacy and significant long-term side effects. Local corticosteroid injection may be considered for peripheral enthesitis or monoarthritis, but NSAIDs remain first-line for axial symptoms. (Source: ARA)

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily with gastroprotection
options.rationale: NSAIDs at full anti-inflammatory doses are first-line treatment for active axial spondyloarthritis. Naproxen or indomethacin are commonly used. A minimum 4-week trial at full dose is required before declaring NSAID failure. Concurrent gastroprotection is appropriate given continuous NSAID use. (Source: ARA; eTG Rheumatology; EULAR/ASAS)

explanation.summary: Axial spondyloarthritis including ankylosing spondylitis is treated with a stepwise approach: NSAIDs first, then biologic agents (TNF inhibitors or IL-17 inhibitors) for NSAID-refractory disease. Conventional DMARDs such as methotrexate and sulfasalazine have no efficacy for axial symptoms. The modified New York criteria require sacroiliitis on imaging plus inflammatory back pain features. HLA-B27 is present in approximately 90% of cases. Regular exercise and physiotherapy are essential non-pharmacological adjuncts.
key_points: NSAIDs at full dose are first-line for axial spondyloarthritis
Conventional DMARDs are ineffective for axial symptoms
PBS requires failure of at least 2 NSAIDs before biologic access
HLA-B27 positive in approximately 90% of AS
explanation.pearls: Continuous NSAID therapy in active ankylosing spondylitis may slow radiographic progression of syndesmophyte formation compared to on-demand use, with a landmark 2-year trial showing significant reduction in modified Stoke AS Spinal Score (mSASSS) progression with continuous celecoxib.

subtopic_detail: Hereditary haemochromatosis - diagnostic pathway
stem: A 48-year-old man of Northern European ancestry presents with fatigue and arthralgia in his second and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. He consumes 2-3 standard drinks on weekends. His GP ordered iron studies which showed markedly elevated transferrin saturation and ferritin.

data_table.Investigations: Fasting transferrin saturation 72% (15-45). Ferritin 1450 mcg/L (30-300). ALT 58 U/L (<40). AST 52 U/L (<35). FBC normal. eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73m2. Fasting glucose 6.8 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Liver biopsy with Perls Prussian blue staining
options.rationale: Liver biopsy was historically the gold standard for diagnosing haemochromatosis, but HFE gene testing has replaced it as the initial diagnostic test. Liver biopsy is now reserved for patients with ferritin >1000 mcg/L or abnormal LFTs to assess for cirrhosis, after genetic confirmation. (Source: GESA Haemochromatosis Guidelines; Haemochromatosis Australia)

options.text: HFE gene testing for C282Y and H63D mutations
options.rationale: HFE gene testing is the recommended diagnostic investigation when transferrin saturation is persistently elevated (>45%). C282Y homozygosity confirms hereditary haemochromatosis in the appropriate clinical context. It is non-invasive and has replaced liver biopsy as the initial diagnostic step. Given his ferritin >1000 and abnormal LFTs, liver assessment will also be needed after gene confirmation. (Source: GESA Haemochromatosis Guidelines; Haemochromatosis Australia)

options.text: MRI liver with T2-star mapping for iron content
options.rationale: FerriScan (MRI R2*) quantifies hepatic iron concentration and is useful for monitoring treatment response or when HFE genotype is negative but iron overload is suspected. However, HFE gene testing is the appropriate first diagnostic step in a patient with suspected hereditary haemochromatosis. (Source: GESA)

options.text: Repeat fasting iron studies in 4 weeks for confirmation
options.rationale: While fasting iron studies should be confirmed on a repeat sample, this patient has a transferrin saturation of 72% with ferritin 1450 and classic clinical features. Proceeding to HFE gene testing is appropriate without further delay, as ferritin >1000 carries risk of organ damage including cirrhosis. (Source: GESA)

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound with hepatic elastography
options.rationale: Ultrasound and elastography assess liver structure and fibrosis but do not establish the diagnosis of haemochromatosis. They may be used as part of liver assessment after the genetic diagnosis is confirmed, particularly to screen for hepatocellular carcinoma in those with cirrhosis. (Source: GESA)

explanation.summary: Hereditary haemochromatosis is the most common genetic condition in Australians of Northern European ancestry, with C282Y homozygosity affecting approximately 1 in 200. Diagnosis is established by HFE gene testing when fasting transferrin saturation is persistently above 45%. Liver assessment for fibrosis is indicated when ferritin exceeds 1000 mcg/L or LFTs are abnormal. Treatment is venesection targeting ferritin 50-100 mcg/L during induction and maintenance. Family screening of first-degree relatives is recommended.
key_points: HFE gene testing is the diagnostic investigation for suspected haemochromatosis
Transferrin saturation >45% is the screening threshold
C282Y homozygosity is the most common genotype in Australian patients
Ferritin >1000 or abnormal LFTs warrant liver assessment for cirrhosis
explanation.pearls: C282Y homozygosity has a phenotypic penetrance of only 28% in males and 1% in females, meaning most homozygotes never develop clinical iron overload, which has important implications for genetic counselling of identified carriers and their families.

subtopic_detail: Adult cardiac arrest - ALS drug timing in shockable rhythm
stem: You are leading a resuscitation for a 55-year-old man who collapsed in the emergency department with witnessed ventricular fibrillation. High-quality CPR was commenced immediately. Two defibrillation shocks have been delivered at 200 J each without return of spontaneous circulation. The rhythm remains ventricular fibrillation.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus after the next shock
options.rationale: Per ANZCOR guidelines, amiodarone 300 mg IV is administered after the third shock, not the second. The current situation is after two shocks. Adrenaline 1 mg IV should be given after the second shock and amiodarone after the third, both during ongoing CPR. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.5)

options.text: Lignocaine 100 mg IV bolus as antiarrhythmic
options.rationale: Lignocaine is an alternative antiarrhythmic if amiodarone is unavailable but is not first-line. Additionally, antiarrhythmics are indicated after the third shock in refractory VF/pVT, and adrenaline should be administered first after the second shock. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.5)

options.text: Adrenaline 1 mg IV bolus with ongoing CPR
options.rationale: Per ANZCOR guidelines, adrenaline 1 mg IV is given after the second shock in a shockable rhythm, then repeated every second loop (every 3-5 minutes). This is the correct pharmacological step at this point. Amiodarone follows after the third shock. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.5; ARC)

options.text: Atropine 3 mg IV bolus for vagal tone
options.rationale: Atropine is no longer recommended in the ALS algorithm for any cardiac arrest rhythm. It was previously used in asystole and PEA but has been removed from ANZCOR guidelines. It has no role in ventricular fibrillation management. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.5)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 5 mmol IV over 2 minutes
options.rationale: IV magnesium is indicated in cardiac arrest only for suspected hypomagnesaemia or torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT with prolonged QT). It is not part of the standard VF/pVT algorithm and should not be used routinely. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.5)

explanation.summary: The ANZCOR ALS algorithm for shockable rhythms (VF/pVT) follows a structured approach: defibrillation with immediate CPR, adrenaline 1 mg IV after the second shock, then amiodarone 300 mg IV after the third shock. Adrenaline is repeated every second loop (every 3-5 minutes). A further dose of amiodarone 150 mg can be given after the fifth shock. Atropine has been removed from the ALS algorithm. Reversible causes (4Hs and 4Ts) should be sought throughout resuscitation.
key_points: Adrenaline 1 mg IV after the second shock in shockable rhythms
Amiodarone 300 mg IV after the third shock
Adrenaline repeated every second loop (every 3-5 minutes)
Atropine is no longer in the ALS algorithm
explanation.pearls: In the ANZCOR shockable rhythm algorithm, adrenaline is given after the second shock (not the first) because early defibrillation alone restores ROSC in a proportion of patients, and the vasoconstrictive effect of adrenaline may paradoxically impair coronary perfusion if given too early.

subtopic_detail: Shock classification - distributive vs cardiogenic discrimination
stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with hypotension. He was found by ambulance with altered consciousness. He has a background of ischaemic heart disease with previous anterior STEMI 3 years ago and chronic heart failure. He has also been feeling unwell with fevers for 2 days. His family reports recent urinary symptoms.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 78/42 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 92% on room air, Temp 39.1 degrees C.
data_table.Examination: Warm peripheries. Bounding pulses. JVP not elevated. Chest: bibasal crackles. Abdomen: suprapubic tenderness. Urine dipstick: leucocytes +++, nitrites positive.
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 5.8 mmol/L. WCC 18.4 x10^9/L. CRP 186 mg/L. Troponin <5 ng/L. NT-proBNP 620 pg/mL (baseline 480). ECG: sinus tachycardia, old Q waves anterior leads.

lead_in: What is the most likely type of shock?

options.text: Cardiogenic shock from acute heart failure exacerbation
options.rationale: Cardiogenic shock presents with cold, clammy peripheries, elevated JVP, and elevated filling pressures. This patient has warm peripheries and bounding pulses with a non-elevated JVP, which is inconsistent with cardiogenic shock. The mildly elevated NT-proBNP is near his baseline and the troponin is negative. (Source: ANZICS; eTG)

options.text: Hypovolaemic shock from inadequate fluid intake
options.rationale: Hypovolaemic shock presents with cold, clammy peripheries, collapsed veins, and tachycardia. This patient has warm peripheries and bounding pulses, which are characteristic of distributive shock. Additionally, the clinical picture with fever and urinary source suggests infection. (Source: ANZICS)

options.text: Obstructive shock from massive pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: Obstructive shock from massive PE typically presents with acute right heart strain - elevated JVP, RV heave, and severe hypoxia disproportionate to chest findings. This patient has warm peripheries, no elevated JVP, and a clear infective source with positive urinary findings. (Source: ANZICS)

options.text: Mixed cardiogenic and hypovolaemic shock pattern
options.rationale: While this patient has chronic heart failure, the haemodynamic profile with warm peripheries and bounding pulses indicates vasodilatory (distributive) physiology, not cardiogenic or hypovolaemic patterns. The near-baseline NT-proBNP and negative troponin argue against acute cardiac decompensation. (Source: ANZICS)

options.text: Distributive shock from urosepsis with warm peripheries
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classic distributive (septic) shock: warm peripheries with bounding pulses (vasodilation), hypotension, fever, tachycardia, elevated lactate, and an identified urinary source. Septic shock is defined by vasopressor requirement despite adequate fluid resuscitation with lactate >2 mmol/L. The haemodynamic profile distinguishes it from cardiogenic shock. (Source: ANZICS; Surviving Sepsis Campaign; eTG)

explanation.summary: Shock is classified into four types based on the underlying haemodynamic mechanism. Distributive (septic) shock is characterised by pathological vasodilation causing warm peripheries, bounding pulses, and low systemic vascular resistance. Cardiogenic shock shows cold peripheries, elevated filling pressures, and low cardiac output. Hypovolaemic shock presents with cold peripheries and collapsed veins. Obstructive shock occurs from mechanical obstruction to cardiac filling or output. The haemodynamic profile, rather than the underlying comorbidities, determines the shock classification.
key_points: Warm peripheries and bounding pulses indicate distributive (septic) shock
Cold, clammy peripheries with elevated JVP suggest cardiogenic shock
Septic shock is defined by vasopressor requirement with lactate >2 despite adequate fluids
Comorbid heart failure does not preclude a primarily septic haemodynamic profile
explanation.pearls: In early septic shock, cardiac output is typically maintained or elevated despite hypotension due to marked reduction in systemic vascular resistance, which is why peripheries remain warm - the 'warm shock' phase transitions to 'cold shock' only in late decompensated sepsis when myocardial depression occurs.

subtopic_detail: Acute viral myocarditis - recognition and initial management
stem: A 26-year-old man presents with central chest pain that has been worsening over 24 hours. He had a coryzal illness 10 days ago. The pain is sharp, pleuritic, and partially relieved by sitting forward. He has no cardiovascular risk factors and no family history of sudden cardiac death.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 37.8 degrees C.
data_table.Investigations: ECG: diffuse ST elevation in leads I, II, aVF, V3-V6 with PR depression. Troponin I 2800 ng/L (<26). NT-proBNP 340 pg/mL (<125). CRP 48 mg/L. FBC normal. Coronary angiography: normal coronary arteries.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: CT coronary angiography with calcium scoring
options.rationale: Conventional coronary angiography has already confirmed normal coronary arteries, excluding ACS. CT coronary angiography would add no diagnostic value. The elevated troponin with normal coronaries points to a non-ischaemic cause requiring tissue characterisation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ; ESC Myocarditis Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat troponin and ECG in 6 hours for trending
options.rationale: While serial troponin monitoring is part of ongoing care, the diagnosis of myocarditis requires tissue characterisation. The troponin is already markedly elevated with normal coronaries, making further trending unnecessary for diagnosis. Cardiac MRI is the key diagnostic investigation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ)

options.text: Endomyocardial biopsy under fluoroscopic guidance
options.rationale: Endomyocardial biopsy is the histological gold standard but is invasive and reserved for cases with haemodynamic compromise, suspected giant cell myocarditis, or when the diagnosis significantly impacts management decisions. Non-invasive cardiac MRI is the appropriate first-line diagnostic investigation for suspected acute myocarditis. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ; ESC Myocarditis Guidelines)

options.text: Cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement
options.rationale: Cardiac MRI is the gold standard non-invasive investigation for acute myocarditis. The Lake Louise criteria use T1 mapping, T2 mapping (oedema), and late gadolinium enhancement (necrosis/fibrosis) to diagnose myocarditis. The pattern of enhancement is typically mid-wall or epicardial, distinguishing it from ischaemic injury. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ; ESC Myocarditis Guidelines)

options.text: Transthoracic echocardiography with strain imaging
options.rationale: While echocardiography is important for assessing ventricular function and excluding pericardial effusion, it cannot definitively diagnose myocarditis. Wall motion abnormalities may be present but are non-specific. Cardiac MRI provides tissue characterisation that echocardiography cannot. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ)

explanation.summary: Acute myocarditis typically presents in young adults following a viral illness with chest pain, troponin elevation, and ECG changes. Cardiac MRI with the updated Lake Louise criteria is the non-invasive gold standard, showing myocardial oedema on T2 mapping and necrosis/fibrosis on late gadolinium enhancement. The pattern is typically non-ischaemic (mid-wall or epicardial). Management is supportive with activity restriction for 3-6 months, monitoring for arrhythmias and heart failure, and avoidance of NSAIDs in the acute phase if ventricular dysfunction is present.
key_points: Cardiac MRI is the non-invasive gold standard for myocarditis diagnosis
Lake Louise criteria: T1/T2 mapping abnormalities plus late gadolinium enhancement
Mid-wall or epicardial LGE pattern distinguishes from ischaemic injury
Activity restriction for minimum 3-6 months is recommended
explanation.pearls: The mid-wall and epicardial late gadolinium enhancement pattern in myocarditis reflects the pathological distribution of viral-mediated inflammation, which spreads from epicardium inward, contrasting with ischaemic injury that begins subendocardially and extends transmurally.

subtopic_detail: Fibromyalgia - diagnosis and non-opioid management
stem: A 44-year-old woman presents with widespread body pain for over 12 months affecting her neck, shoulders, back, and both legs. She also reports significant fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive difficulties she describes as 'brain fog'. She has seen multiple specialists without a diagnosis. She has no joint swelling or morning stiffness lasting beyond 15 minutes. She has tried paracetamol with minimal benefit.

data_table.Examination: Multiple tender points at bilateral trapezius, lateral epicondyles, greater trochanters, and medial fat pads of knees. No joint synovitis, swelling, or effusion. Full range of movement at all joints. Neurological examination normal.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CRP, ESR, TFTs, ANA, RF, anti-CCP - all normal. HbA1c 5.2%.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.text: Tramadol 50 mg four times daily for pain control
options.rationale: Opioids including tramadol are not recommended for fibromyalgia as they have limited efficacy and carry significant risks of dependence and hyperalgesia. Australian and international guidelines explicitly advise against opioid use in this condition. (Source: eTG Pain; RACGP; EULAR Fibromyalgia Recommendations)

options.text: Prednisolone 15 mg daily for a 4-week therapeutic trial
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no role in fibromyalgia management as there is no inflammatory pathology. A trial of prednisolone would expose the patient to unnecessary side effects without benefit. Normal inflammatory markers and absence of synovitis exclude inflammatory arthropathy. (Source: eTG Pain; RACGP)

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily with gastroprotection
options.rationale: NSAIDs have limited efficacy in fibromyalgia as the pain mechanism is central sensitisation rather than peripheral inflammation. They are not recommended as primary therapy and carry GI and renal risks with chronic use. (Source: eTG Pain; EULAR Fibromyalgia Recommendations)

options.text: Referral to a rheumatologist for further investigation
options.rationale: The clinical picture is consistent with fibromyalgia, and all relevant investigations to exclude inflammatory and autoimmune conditions are normal. Further specialist investigation is unlikely to yield a different diagnosis. The focus should be on initiating evidence-based treatment. (Source: RACGP)

options.text: Graduated exercise programme with amitriptyline at night
options.rationale: Graduated aerobic exercise is the most strongly evidence-based intervention for fibromyalgia and should be combined with patient education. Low-dose amitriptyline (10-25 mg nocte) is the first-line pharmacological agent, improving pain, sleep, and fatigue. This combined approach is recommended by Australian and international guidelines. (Source: eTG Pain; RACGP; EULAR Fibromyalgia Recommendations)

explanation.summary: Fibromyalgia is a central sensitisation syndrome diagnosed clinically by widespread pain lasting more than 3 months with associated fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive dysfunction, in the absence of another cause. The 2010 ACR criteria use the widespread pain index and symptom severity scale. Management is multimodal: patient education, graduated exercise, and pharmacotherapy. Amitriptyline, pregabalin, and duloxetine are first-line pharmacological options. Opioids, NSAIDs, and corticosteroids are ineffective and not recommended.
key_points: Diagnosed clinically: widespread pain >3 months, fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive symptoms
Normal inflammatory markers, autoimmune serology, and examination exclude mimics
Graduated aerobic exercise is the strongest evidence-based intervention
Amitriptyline, pregabalin, or duloxetine are first-line pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: Graduated aerobic exercise (e.g. walking, swimming) at moderate intensity for 30 minutes 2-3 times per week produces a number needed to treat of 5-6 for clinically meaningful pain reduction in fibromyalgia, with benefit maintained at 12-month follow-up when exercise is continued.

subtopic_detail: Denosumab cessation - rebound vertebral fracture risk
stem: A 72-year-old woman has been on denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 6 months for 5 years for osteoporosis, with good response. She wishes to cease treatment due to needle anxiety. Her most recent DXA shows T-score -1.8 at the lumbar spine, improved from -3.2 at initiation. She has no history of fragility fractures. Her eGFR is 52 mL/min/1.73m2.

data_table.Investigations: DXA: T-score -1.8 lumbar spine, -1.5 femoral neck. Calcium 2.38 mmol/L. 25-OH vitamin D 72 nmol/L. eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m2.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Transition to oral alendronate before ceasing denosumab
options.rationale: Abrupt denosumab cessation causes rapid bone loss with rebound vertebral fractures, sometimes multiple, within 12-18 months. Transitioning to an oral bisphosphonate (alendronate) for at least 12 months consolidates gains and prevents rebound. This is the recommended strategy per current expert consensus and ANZBMS guidance. (Source: ANZBMS Position Statement; eTG; Osteoporosis Australia)

options.text: Cease denosumab immediately given improved bone density
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of denosumab without bisphosphonate transition is associated with rapid rebound bone loss and a significantly elevated risk of multiple vertebral fractures within 7-18 months. The improved T-score does not protect against this rebound effect, which can reduce BMD below pre-treatment levels. (Source: ANZBMS Position Statement)

options.text: Continue denosumab indefinitely as cessation is unsafe
options.rationale: While denosumab cessation carries risk, indefinite therapy has its own concerns including atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw with very long-term use. Safe transition to bisphosphonate therapy is achievable and is the preferred strategy when a patient wishes to cease. (Source: ANZBMS; eTG)

options.text: Transition to zoledronic acid given her renal impairment
options.rationale: Zoledronic acid is an appropriate transition agent from denosumab, but it is contraindicated when eGFR is below 35 mL/min/1.73m2 and used with caution when eGFR is 35-60. With her eGFR of 52, oral alendronate is preferred. The dose and formulation of zoledronic acid for osteoporosis differs from the oncology formulation. (Source: AMH; eTG)

options.text: Taper denosumab to annual dosing over the next 2 years
options.rationale: There is no evidence supporting tapering of denosumab dosing frequency. Extending the dosing interval beyond 6 months allows RANKL-mediated bone resorption to resume, potentially triggering the same rebound effect as cessation. The recommended approach is bisphosphonate transition, not dose tapering. (Source: ANZBMS Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Denosumab is a RANKL inhibitor whose effect is fully reversible upon cessation. Abrupt discontinuation triggers a rebound increase in osteoclast activity that can cause rapid bone loss exceeding pre-treatment levels and multiple vertebral fractures within 7-18 months. The recommended strategy for ceasing denosumab is transition to oral bisphosphonate therapy (usually alendronate) for at least 12 months starting at the time the next denosumab injection would have been due. Monitoring with repeat DXA and bone turnover markers guides adequacy of the transition.
key_points: Denosumab cessation without transition causes rebound vertebral fractures
Transition to oral bisphosphonate for >=12 months is the recommended strategy
Bone loss after denosumab cessation can exceed pre-treatment levels
Dose tapering or extended intervals are not evidence-based alternatives
explanation.pearls: Post-denosumab rebound involves a surge in bone resorption markers to 2-3 times baseline levels, peaking at 6 months after the missed dose, with the risk of multiple spontaneous vertebral fractures highest between months 7 and 18 after cessation.

subtopic_detail: Hypercalcaemia workup - PTH-dependent vs PTH-independent
stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with 3 months of fatigue, polyuria, and constipation. She has a history of recurrent nephrolithiasis. She takes no calcium supplements or vitamin D. She has no known malignancy. There is no family history of endocrine disorders.

data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 2.92 mmol/L (2.15-2.55). Phosphate 0.68 mmol/L (0.80-1.50). PTH 12.8 pmol/L (1.6-6.9). 25-OH vitamin D 48 nmol/L. eGFR 62 mL/min/1.73m2. 24-hour urinary calcium 9.2 mmol/day (2.5-7.5).

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy from PTHrP
options.rationale: In humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy, PTHrP suppresses endogenous PTH production. The PTH level would be low or suppressed, not elevated as in this case. The elevated PTH with hypercalcaemia confirms a PTH-dependent cause. (Source: eTG Endocrinology; Endocrine Society Guidelines)

options.text: Vitamin D intoxication from excess supplementation
options.rationale: Vitamin D intoxication causes PTH-independent hypercalcaemia with suppressed PTH. The patient's 25-OH vitamin D is 48 nmol/L (not elevated) and she takes no supplements. The elevated PTH excludes vitamin D-mediated hypercalcaemia. (Source: eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: Primary hyperparathyroidism from parathyroid adenoma
options.rationale: Hypercalcaemia with an inappropriately elevated PTH (should be suppressed by high calcium) confirms PTH-dependent hypercalcaemia. Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause, typically from a solitary parathyroid adenoma (85%). The low phosphate and elevated urinary calcium support this. Recurrent nephrolithiasis is a classic complication. (Source: eTG Endocrinology; Endocrine Society; AACE/ACE Guidelines)

options.text: Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia from CaSR mutation
options.rationale: FHH is a benign condition caused by calcium-sensing receptor mutation. It presents with mild hypercalcaemia, elevated PTH, but characteristically LOW urinary calcium excretion (calcium-creatinine clearance ratio <0.01). This patient has elevated urinary calcium (9.2 mmol/day), effectively excluding FHH. (Source: Endocrine Society; eTG)

options.text: Granulomatous disease with extrarenal calcitriol production
options.rationale: Sarcoidosis and other granulomatous diseases cause hypercalcaemia through unregulated 1-alpha-hydroxylase in granulomata, producing excess calcitriol. This results in PTH-independent hypercalcaemia with suppressed PTH. The elevated PTH in this case excludes granulomatous disease as the cause. (Source: eTG Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemia workup begins with PTH measurement to distinguish PTH-dependent from PTH-independent causes. Elevated or inappropriately normal PTH with hypercalcaemia indicates primary hyperparathyroidism (most common) or familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia. These are differentiated by 24-hour urinary calcium or calcium-creatinine clearance ratio. Primary hyperparathyroidism is caused by a solitary adenoma in 85%, multigland hyperplasia in 10-15%, and carcinoma in <1%. Surgical parathyroidectomy is indicated for symptomatic disease, calcium >0.25 mmol/L above normal, renal stones, eGFR <60, or age <50.
key_points: PTH elevated with hypercalcaemia = PTH-dependent cause (PHPT vs FHH)
Urinary calcium distinguishes PHPT (elevated) from FHH (low)
PHPT most commonly caused by solitary parathyroid adenoma (85%)
Surgery indicated for symptomatic disease, calcium >0.25 above ULN, stones, or age <50
explanation.pearls: The calcium-creatinine clearance ratio (CCCR) is calculated as (urine calcium x serum creatinine) / (serum calcium x urine creatinine), with a value <0.01 strongly suggesting FHH and a value >0.02 supporting PHPT, making it the key discriminator between these two PTH-dependent conditions.

subtopic_detail: Drug-induced vs pathological hyperprolactinaemia - discrimination
stem: A 32-year-old woman on risperidone 4 mg daily for schizophrenia presents with galactorrhoea and amenorrhoea for 6 months. She is sexually active and a pregnancy test is negative. Her psychiatrist requests a prolactin level, which returns markedly elevated. Previous prolactin levels before starting risperidone were not available.

data_table.Investigations: Prolactin 4200 mU/L (<500). TSH 2.1 mU/L (0.4-4.0). Beta-hCG negative. LH 1.2 IU/L. FSH 2.4 IU/L. Renal function normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease risperidone and recheck prolactin in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Abruptly ceasing an antipsychotic in a patient with schizophrenia is inappropriate without psychiatric collaboration and may precipitate psychotic relapse. Additionally, risperidone-induced hyperprolactinaemia typically produces levels below 3000 mU/L. A level of 4200 mU/L raises concern for a prolactinoma requiring imaging before medication changes. (Source: RANZCP CPG; eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: MRI pituitary with gadolinium contrast enhancement
options.rationale: A prolactin level above 3000-4000 mU/L exceeds the expected range for drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia (typically <2000-3000 mU/L with risperidone). MRI pituitary is indicated to exclude a prolactinoma. Macroprolactinomas cause levels proportional to tumour size, and a concurrent prolactinoma on risperidone must be excluded before attributing the elevation solely to the drug. (Source: eTG Endocrinology; Endocrine Society; RANZCP)

options.text: Switch to aripiprazole which lowers prolactin levels
options.rationale: While aripiprazole is a partial dopamine agonist that can reduce prolactin, switching antipsychotic before imaging is premature. The prolactin level of 4200 mU/L is above the expected range for drug-induced elevation and a prolactinoma must be excluded first. If a macroprolactinoma is present, management differs significantly. (Source: RANZCP; AMH)

options.text: Measure macroprolactin to exclude laboratory artefact
options.rationale: Macroprolactinaemia (big-big prolactin) is a common cause of mildly elevated prolactin detected on immunoassay that is biologically inactive. However, it typically presents with incidentally elevated prolactin without symptoms. This patient has clinical symptoms (galactorrhoea, amenorrhoea) and a significantly elevated level, making macroprolactin less likely as the sole explanation. Imaging is the priority. (Source: eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: Add cabergoline 0.25 mg weekly to reduce prolactin
options.rationale: Adding a dopamine agonist (cabergoline) to a dopamine antagonist (risperidone) is pharmacologically counterproductive and may destabilise psychiatric control. The appropriate step is first to image the pituitary to establish the cause of the markedly elevated prolactin, then determine management in collaboration with endocrinology and psychiatry. (Source: eTG Endocrinology; RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Hyperprolactinaemia in the context of antipsychotic use presents a diagnostic challenge. Typical antipsychotic-induced prolactin elevation from risperidone ranges up to 2000-3000 mU/L. Levels exceeding this threshold warrant MRI pituitary to exclude a coexisting prolactinoma. The magnitude of prolactin elevation generally correlates with tumour size in macroprolactinomas. Management requires collaboration between endocrinology and psychiatry, as dopamine agonist therapy for prolactinoma conflicts with antipsychotic dopamine blockade.
key_points: Drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia typically produces levels below 2000-3000 mU/L
Prolactin above 4000 mU/L warrants MRI pituitary regardless of medication use
Risperidone causes the most prolactin elevation among antipsychotics
Management requires endocrinology-psychiatry collaboration
explanation.pearls: Prolactin levels >10,000 mU/L are virtually diagnostic of macroprolactinoma, while the 'hook effect' (falsely low prolactin on immunoassay due to antibody saturation) can occur with very large tumours and should be suspected when a large pituitary mass has only mildly elevated prolactin - requesting serial dilution corrects this artefact.

subtopic_detail: Acromegaly - biochemical confirmation pathway
stem: A 46-year-old man is referred with increasing ring and shoe size over 3 years. He reports headaches, excessive sweating, and joint pain. He was recently diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. His dentist noted widening interdental spaces. On examination he has coarse facial features, macroglossia, and doughy hands.

data_table.Investigations: IGF-1 68 nmol/L (12-38 for age and sex). Random GH 8.2 mcg/L. HbA1c 7.4%. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fasting glucose 8.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: 24-hour GH sampling with mean level calculation
options.rationale: Twenty-four-hour GH profiles are research tools and are not used in clinical practice for diagnosing acromegaly. The oral glucose tolerance test with GH suppression is the standard confirmatory test. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly Guidelines; eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: MRI pituitary with gadolinium as the definitive test
options.rationale: MRI pituitary is essential for tumour localisation but is performed after biochemical confirmation, not as the confirmatory test. A pituitary incidentaloma could coexist with normal GH dynamics, so biochemical confirmation must precede imaging to establish the diagnosis. (Source: Endocrine Society; eTG)

options.text: Repeat IGF-1 in 4 weeks to confirm persistent elevation
options.rationale: While IGF-1 is the initial screening test and is elevated here, the diagnosis requires dynamic testing to confirm autonomous GH secretion. Repeating IGF-1 delays the diagnosis unnecessarily when the clinical picture and elevated IGF-1 already warrant confirmatory testing. (Source: Endocrine Society)

options.text: Insulin tolerance test to assess GH reserve dynamics
options.rationale: The insulin tolerance test assesses GH deficiency (stimulation) and cortisol reserve, not GH excess. In acromegaly, the confirmatory test is GH suppression with oral glucose. The insulin tolerance test is contraindicated in patients with epilepsy or ischaemic heart disease and is not the appropriate test here. (Source: Endocrine Society)

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test with GH suppression profile
options.rationale: The 75 g oral glucose tolerance test with GH measurement is the gold standard confirmatory test for acromegaly. In normal individuals, GH suppresses to <0.4 mcg/L after oral glucose. Failure to suppress confirms autonomous GH secretion. This is performed after an elevated IGF-1 and before pituitary MRI. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly Guidelines; eTG Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Acromegaly is caused by excess growth hormone, most commonly from a pituitary somatotroph adenoma. The diagnostic pathway begins with elevated IGF-1 (age and sex-adjusted), followed by confirmatory oral glucose tolerance test showing failure of GH suppression below 0.4 mcg/L. MRI pituitary with gadolinium then localises the tumour. Treatment is transsphenoidal surgery for most patients, with medical therapy (somatostatin analogues, pegvisomant, cabergoline) reserved for unresectable or persistent disease.
