Complications include diabetes, hypertension, cardiomyopathy, and increased colorectal cancer risk.
key_points: IGF-1 is the initial screening test for acromegaly
75 g OGTT with GH levels is the confirmatory test - failure to suppress below 0.4 mcg/L is diagnostic
MRI pituitary follows biochemical confirmation
Colonoscopy screening is recommended due to increased polyp and cancer risk
explanation.pearls: The GH suppression threshold was lowered from 1.0 to 0.4 mcg/L (and some guidelines suggest <0.3 mcg/L) with the introduction of ultrasensitive GH assays, meaning historic cut-offs may underdiagnose acromegaly, particularly in patients with mild autonomous secretion.

subtopic_detail: Frontotemporal dementia - behavioural variant recognition
stem: A 58-year-old man is brought by his wife who reports progressive personality change over 18 months. He has become disinhibited, making inappropriate sexual comments to strangers, and recently shoplifted despite no financial difficulty. He has developed a compulsive fixation on eating sweet foods and has gained 12 kg. He shows no concern about his behaviour and lacks insight. His memory for recent events remains relatively intact. His father had a progressive neurological condition diagnosed in his 50s.

data_table.Examination: MMSE 26/30 (lost points for attention and visuospatial). Primitive reflexes present bilaterally (grasp reflex, palmomental). No parkinsonism. No motor neurone signs. Speech fluent but tangential.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs, B12 - normal. MRI brain: bilateral frontal and anterior temporal lobe atrophy, greater on the right. No vascular changes.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Late-onset bipolar disorder with manic features
options.rationale: While disinhibition and impulsivity can mimic mania, the progressive insidious course over 18 months, primitive reflexes, frontal and temporal atrophy on MRI, and preserved memory distinguish this from bipolar disorder. Bipolar disorder presents with episodic mood disturbance, not progressive structural brain changes. (Source: NHMRC Dementia Guidelines; eTG Neurology)

options.text: Early-onset Alzheimer disease with behavioural variant
options.rationale: Alzheimer disease typically presents with prominent episodic memory impairment early in the course. This patient has relatively preserved memory with prominent behavioural and personality changes, which is the hallmark of frontotemporal dementia. The pattern of frontal and anterior temporal atrophy on MRI also favours FTD over Alzheimer disease. (Source: NHMRC Dementia Guidelines)

options.text: Dementia with Lewy bodies presenting with disinhibition
options.rationale: DLB characteristically presents with fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism. Disinhibition and personality change are not core features. This patient lacks hallucinations and parkinsonism, and the MRI shows frontal atrophy rather than the more diffuse pattern of DLB. (Source: NHMRC Dementia Guidelines)

options.text: Behavioural variant frontotemporal dementia
options.rationale: The progressive disinhibition, loss of empathy, compulsive eating behaviour, lack of insight, and relatively preserved memory are classic features of behavioural variant FTD. The MRI showing bilateral frontal and anterior temporal atrophy is the characteristic imaging pattern. Primitive reflexes reflect frontal lobe dysfunction. Onset before 65 and positive family history are typical for FTD, which has a genetic component in 30-40% of cases. (Source: NHMRC Dementia Guidelines; eTG Neurology; Rascovsky criteria)

options.text: Vascular dementia from subcortical ischaemic disease
options.rationale: Vascular dementia presents with stepwise cognitive decline and focal neurological signs, with white matter changes on MRI. This patient has progressive behavioural change with frontal and temporal atrophy and no vascular changes on MRI, which is inconsistent with vascular dementia. (Source: NHMRC Dementia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Behavioural variant frontotemporal dementia is characterised by progressive deterioration in personality and social conduct with relative preservation of memory in the early stages. The Rascovsky criteria require at least three of: disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsive behaviours, hyperorality/dietary changes, and executive dysfunction. Onset is typically before age 65. MRI shows frontal and anterior temporal atrophy. Approximately 30-40% of cases have a genetic basis, with mutations in MAPT, GRN, or C9orf72 being most common. There are no disease-modifying treatments; management is supportive.
key_points: Behavioural change and personality deterioration precede memory loss in bvFTD
Rascovsky criteria require >=3 of 6 behavioural features
Frontal and anterior temporal atrophy on MRI is characteristic
30-40% of FTD cases have a genetic component
explanation.pearls: The C9orf72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion is the most common genetic cause of both FTD and motor neurone disease, and up to 15% of patients with bvFTD develop concurrent MND features, making neurological examination for upper and lower motor neurone signs essential at every review.

subtopic_detail: Motor neurone disease - clinical recognition and differentiation
stem: A 62-year-old man presents with progressive difficulty gripping objects with his right hand over 8 months. He has noticed fasciculations in his forearms and thighs. He has developed slurred speech in the past 2 months. He denies sensory symptoms, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or neck pain. MRI cervical spine performed by his GP was normal.

data_table.Examination: Wasting and fasciculations of right thenar eminence and first dorsal interosseous. Brisk reflexes in all four limbs including jaw jerk. Bilateral extensor plantar responses. Tongue fasciculations with mild wasting. Sensation intact throughout. Cerebellar examination normal.
data_table.Investigations: MRI cervical spine: no cord compression or myelopathy signal. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs, CRP, glucose - normal. CK mildly elevated at 380 U/L (<220).

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with bulbar involvement
options.rationale: The combination of upper motor neurone signs (brisk reflexes, extensor plantars, spastic jaw jerk) and lower motor neurone signs (wasting, fasciculations) in multiple regions (limbs and bulbar) without sensory involvement is pathognomonic for ALS. The normal cervical MRI excludes structural causes. Bulbar involvement (dysarthria, tongue fasciculations) indicates bulbar-onset MND superimposed on limb-onset disease. (Source: eTG Neurology; MND Australia)

options.text: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy with radiculopathy
options.rationale: Cervical myelopathy can produce upper motor neurone signs in the lower limbs and lower motor neurone signs in the upper limbs, but the cervical MRI is normal, excluding this diagnosis. Additionally, cervical myelopathy does not explain the bulbar signs (dysarthria, tongue fasciculations and wasting) or the widespread fasciculations. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Multifocal motor neuropathy with conduction block
options.rationale: Multifocal motor neuropathy presents with asymmetric distal weakness and wasting without upper motor neurone signs. This patient has prominent UMN signs (brisk reflexes, extensor plantars, brisk jaw jerk) which are absent in MMN. MMN is also treatable with IVIG, making the distinction important. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Kennedy disease with X-linked bulbospinal atrophy
options.rationale: Kennedy disease is an X-linked lower motor neurone disorder presenting with bulbar and limb weakness, fasciculations, and gynecomastia. Crucially, it does not produce upper motor neurone signs. This patient's brisk reflexes, extensor plantars, and brisk jaw jerk are UMN features that exclude Kennedy disease. (Source: eTG Neurology)

options.text: Myasthenia gravis with progressive generalised weakness
options.rationale: Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness without wasting or fasciculations, and reflexes are typically normal or reduced. This patient has prominent wasting, fasciculations, and upper motor neurone signs, none of which are features of myasthenia. MG also does not cause extensor plantars or brisk jaw jerk. (Source: eTG Neurology)

explanation.summary: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is diagnosed clinically by the presence of concurrent upper and lower motor neurone signs in multiple body regions without sensory involvement. The revised El Escorial criteria require UMN and LMN degeneration spreading within a region and to other regions. EMG/nerve conduction studies confirm widespread denervation. Median survival from symptom onset is 2-5 years, with bulbar-onset carrying a worse prognosis. Riluzole extends survival by approximately 2-3 months. Multidisciplinary care involving neurology, respiratory, speech pathology, occupational therapy, and palliative care is essential.
key_points: Concurrent UMN and LMN signs in multiple regions without sensory involvement is pathognomonic
Normal cervical MRI is essential to exclude structural mimics
Bulbar involvement indicated by dysarthria, dysphagia, and tongue fasciculations
EMG showing widespread acute and chronic denervation supports the diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Fasciculations in the tongue are highly specific for ALS when accompanied by tongue wasting, as benign fasciculation syndrome almost never involves the tongue, making this a critical discriminating sign during examination.

subtopic_detail: DRESS syndrome - recognition and discrimination from SJS/TEN
stem: A 54-year-old man presents with a widespread morbilliform rash, facial oedema, and fever 5 weeks after starting allopurinol 300 mg daily for gout. He has cervical lymphadenopathy. He reports no mucosal ulceration or skin blistering.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 132/78 mmHg, Temp 38.8 degrees C.
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 14.8 x10^9/L with eosinophils 3.2 x10^9/L (0.0-0.5). ALT 286 U/L (<40). AST 198 U/L (<35). ALP 142 U/L (30-110). Bilirubin 22 micromol/L (<20). Creatinine 138 micromol/L (60-110). CRP 86 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome from allopurinol exposure
options.rationale: SJS presents with mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital erosions), target lesions, and epidermal detachment, typically 1-3 weeks after drug exposure. This patient has no mucosal involvement, no skin blistering, has eosinophilia and hepatitis, and the 5-week latency is characteristic of DRESS rather than SJS. (Source: eTG Dermatology; ACD)

options.text: Viral exanthem with coincidental liver derangement
options.rationale: While viral exanthems can cause morbilliform rashes with fever, the marked eosinophilia (3.2 x10^9/L), significant hepatitis, facial oedema, and temporal relationship to allopurinol initiation make drug reaction far more likely. Viral exanthems do not typically cause this degree of eosinophilia or organ involvement. (Source: eTG Dermatology)

options.text: Acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis from allopurinol
options.rationale: AGEP presents with acute onset non-follicular sterile pustules on an erythematous base, typically within 48 hours of drug exposure. This patient has a morbilliform rash without pustules, onset 5 weeks after drug initiation, and significant eosinophilia and organ involvement, which are features of DRESS rather than AGEP. (Source: eTG Dermatology)

options.text: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
options.rationale: DRESS (also called drug-induced hypersensitivity syndrome) presents with morbilliform rash, facial oedema, fever, eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, and internal organ involvement (hepatitis most common). The 2-8 week latency from drug initiation is characteristic. Allopurinol is one of the most common causative agents. The RegiSCAR criteria are used for diagnosis. (Source: eTG Dermatology; ACD; RegiSCAR Criteria)

options.text: Serum sickness-like reaction to xanthine oxidase inhibitor
options.rationale: Serum sickness-like reaction presents with fever, rash, arthralgia, and lymphadenopathy but typically lacks the marked eosinophilia and significant hepatitis seen here. The degree of organ involvement and the specific pattern of eosinophilia with facial oedema is more consistent with DRESS than serum sickness. (Source: eTG Dermatology)

explanation.summary: DRESS syndrome is a severe drug hypersensitivity reaction characterised by a triad of rash, eosinophilia, and internal organ involvement (most commonly hepatitis). It has a characteristic latency of 2-8 weeks from drug initiation, which distinguishes it from SJS/TEN (1-3 weeks) and AGEP (days). The most common causative drugs in Australia are allopurinol, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), and sulfonamides. Management involves immediate drug cessation and systemic corticosteroids. HHV-6 reactivation is detected in many cases. DRESS carries a mortality of 5-10%, primarily from hepatic failure.
key_points: DRESS triad: rash + eosinophilia + organ involvement (usually hepatitis)
Latency 2-8 weeks distinguishes DRESS from SJS/TEN (1-3 weeks) and AGEP (days)
Allopurinol is among the most common causative agents in Australia
Facial oedema and lymphadenopathy are characteristic features
explanation.pearls: HLA-B*5801 testing before starting allopurinol is recommended in patients of South-East Asian, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, and African-American ancestry due to a strong association with allopurinol-induced DRESS and SJS/TEN, with the PBS now subsidising this pharmacogenomic test in at-risk populations.

subtopic_detail: Incidental pulmonary nodule - risk assessment and surveillance
stem: A 62-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history has a CT chest performed for investigation of rib pain after a fall. A solitary solid pulmonary nodule measuring 7 mm is incidentally found in the right upper lobe. There is no mediastinal lymphadenopathy. He has no previous CT imaging for comparison. He has no history of malignancy and no constitutional symptoms.

data_table.Investigations: CT chest: 7 mm solid nodule right upper lobe, smooth borders, no calcification. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. Rib fractures 9th and 10th right.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of the incidental pulmonary nodule?

options.text: Immediate CT-guided biopsy for tissue diagnosis
options.rationale: CT-guided biopsy of a 7 mm nodule carries significant complication risk (pneumothorax 15-25%) with a high rate of non-diagnostic sampling. For a nodule of this size without clearly malignant features, surveillance CT is the appropriate initial approach. Biopsy is reserved for growing nodules or those not amenable to surveillance. (Source: Fleischner Society; TSANZ)

options.text: Follow-up CT chest at 6-12 months and then annually
options.rationale: Per Fleischner Society recommendations, a solid nodule measuring 6-8 mm in a high-risk patient (>30 pack-year smoking history) warrants follow-up CT at 6-12 months, then at 18-24 months if stable. Growth is assessed by volume or diameter change. Stability over 2 years of surveillance generally indicates benignity. (Source: Fleischner Society Guidelines 2017; TSANZ)

options.text: PET-CT scan for metabolic characterisation of nodule
options.rationale: PET-CT has limited sensitivity for nodules smaller than 8-10 mm due to partial volume effects, resulting in a high false-negative rate. It is more useful for characterising nodules greater than 8-10 mm or for staging confirmed malignancy. Surveillance CT is preferred for 7 mm nodules. (Source: Fleischner Society Guidelines; TSANZ)

options.text: No follow-up needed as nodule is below the size threshold
options.rationale: Fleischner Society guidelines recommend no follow-up for solid nodules <6 mm in low-risk patients, but this is a 7 mm nodule in a high-risk patient (30 pack-year smoking history). Follow-up CT at 6-12 months is recommended. Dismissing a 7 mm nodule in a smoker would risk missing an early lung cancer. (Source: Fleischner Society Guidelines)

options.text: Referral for video-assisted thoracoscopic resection
options.rationale: Surgical resection without prior characterisation is inappropriate for a 7 mm nodule with smooth borders. The probability of malignancy for a 7 mm solid nodule is approximately 1-5%. Surveillance is the standard approach, with resection reserved for growing nodules or those with clearly malignant features on interval imaging. (Source: Fleischner Society Guidelines; TSANZ)

explanation.summary: The 2017 Fleischner Society guidelines provide a risk-stratified approach to incidental pulmonary nodule management. For solid nodules 6-8 mm, management depends on patient risk: low-risk patients may have a single follow-up CT at 6-12 months, while high-risk patients require follow-up at 6-12 months then again at 18-24 months. Nodules >8 mm warrant either 3-month follow-up CT, PET-CT, or tissue sampling. Risk factors include smoking history, upper lobe location, spiculated margins, and emphysema. Volume doubling time assessment on serial CT is more sensitive than diameter change.
key_points: Fleischner guidelines stratify management by nodule size and patient risk
Solid 6-8 mm nodule in high-risk patient: CT at 6-12 months then 18-24 months
PET-CT has limited sensitivity below 8-10 mm due to partial volume effects
Stability over 2 years generally indicates benignity
explanation.pearls: Volume doubling time of 400 days or less on serial CT is highly suspicious for malignancy for solid nodules, and modern volumetric CT software can detect 25% volume increases that correspond to only 8% diameter increases, far exceeding the sensitivity of manual calliper measurements.

subtopic_detail: Elevated PSA - interpretation and further investigation
stem: A 64-year-old man presents after a routine health check revealed a PSA of 6.8 mcg/L. He has no urinary symptoms, no family history of prostate cancer, and no previous PSA testing. Digital rectal examination performed by his GP was normal. He takes finasteride 5 mg daily for male pattern hair loss.

data_table.Investigations: PSA 6.8 mcg/L (age-adjusted reference <4.5 for 60-69). Free-to-total PSA ratio 12%. Previous PSA: none available. DRE: smooth, non-tender prostate, no nodules.

lead_in: What is the most important consideration when interpreting this PSA result?

options.text: Repeat PSA in 6 weeks to confirm persistent elevation
options.rationale: While repeat PSA may be appropriate to confirm elevation, the most important consideration is that finasteride halves PSA levels. A PSA of 6.8 on finasteride effectively represents a level of approximately 13.6 mcg/L off finasteride, which is markedly elevated and requires urgent further investigation rather than watchful waiting. (Source: USANZ/PCFA; AMH)

options.text: The normal DRE excludes clinically significant cancer
options.rationale: A normal DRE does not exclude prostate cancer. Approximately 25-30% of prostate cancers are not palpable on DRE, particularly in the anterior prostate or when the tumour is small. PSA elevation requires further investigation regardless of DRE findings. (Source: USANZ/PCFA Guidelines; RACGP)

options.text: Finasteride halves PSA so the true level is approximately double
options.rationale: 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) reduce PSA by approximately 50% after 6-12 months of use. The measured PSA of 6.8 mcg/L should be doubled to approximately 13.6 mcg/L to estimate the true level, which is significantly elevated and warrants urgent urological referral for multiparametric MRI and probable biopsy. (Source: USANZ/PCFA Guidelines; AMH; RACGP PSA Testing Guidelines)

options.text: The low free-to-total ratio suggests benign prostatic cause
options.rationale: A free-to-total PSA ratio below 15-25% actually increases the probability of prostate cancer. A ratio of 12% is concerning and supports further investigation. A higher free-to-total ratio (>25%) would be more reassuring and suggestive of benign prostatic hyperplasia. (Source: USANZ/PCFA Guidelines)

options.text: Age-adjusted reference range makes this result acceptable
options.rationale: While age-adjusted PSA ranges exist, the critical issue is that finasteride halves the measured PSA. The true level is approximately 13.6 mcg/L, which exceeds age-adjusted thresholds by a significant margin. Additionally, the low free-to-total ratio of 12% further increases concern for malignancy. (Source: USANZ/PCFA Guidelines; RACGP)

explanation.summary: PSA interpretation requires consideration of factors that alter PSA levels. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) reduce PSA by approximately 50% after 6-12 months of use, so the measured PSA should be doubled to estimate the true level. Other factors affecting PSA include recent DRE (minimal effect), UTI, prostatitis, ejaculation within 48 hours, and cycling. The free-to-total PSA ratio helps discriminate between benign and malignant causes, with ratios below 15% increasing the probability of cancer. Multiparametric MRI prostate is now recommended before biopsy.
key_points: Finasteride/dutasteride reduce PSA by approximately 50% - double the measured value
Free-to-total PSA ratio <15% increases probability of malignancy
Normal DRE does not exclude prostate cancer
Multiparametric MRI prostate is recommended before biopsy
explanation.pearls: The PSA halving effect of finasteride reaches steady state at approximately 6 months of therapy, and any subsequent PSA rise while on finasteride should be treated with heightened suspicion as it may indicate early prostate cancer growth overcoming the drug effect.

subtopic_detail: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis vs hypersensitivity pneumonitis - discrimination
stem: A 68-year-old retired farmer presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea and dry cough over 18 months. He is a lifelong non-smoker. He kept racing pigeons for 25 years until 5 years ago. He takes no medications known to cause pulmonary fibrosis. On examination he has bilateral fine inspiratory crackles at the lung bases and digital clubbing.

data_table.Investigations: Spirometry: FVC 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC ratio 0.84. DLCO 48% predicted. HRCT chest: bilateral ground-glass opacification with mosaic attenuation predominantly in upper and mid-zones, with areas of fibrotic change (traction bronchiectasis). No honeycombing. Serum avian precipitins positive. ANA negative.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis with UIP pattern
options.rationale: IPF characteristically shows a UIP pattern on HRCT with basal-predominant honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and minimal ground-glass. This patient has upper and mid-zone predominant disease with mosaic attenuation and ground-glass, which is atypical for IPF. The history of pigeon exposure and positive avian precipitins point to chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis. (Source: TSANZ ILD Guidelines; ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT IPF Guidelines)

options.text: Non-specific interstitial pneumonia from unknown cause
options.rationale: NSIP shows bilateral ground-glass with relative subpleural sparing, and is often associated with connective tissue disease. This patient has mosaic attenuation (air trapping) and positive avian precipitins with a clear exposure history, which specifically identifies chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis. (Source: TSANZ)

options.text: Sarcoidosis with pulmonary fibrosis progression
options.rationale: While sarcoidosis can cause upper and mid-zone disease, it typically shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomata. The mosaic attenuation pattern, pigeon exposure history, and positive avian precipitins are not features of sarcoidosis. Stage IV sarcoidosis presents with established fibrosis without ground-glass. (Source: TSANZ)

options.text: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis from avian antigens
options.rationale: The combination of significant avian antigen exposure (pigeon fancier for 25 years), upper and mid-zone predominant ground-glass with mosaic attenuation on HRCT (reflecting small airways disease), fibrotic changes, restrictive spirometry, markedly reduced DLCO, and positive avian precipitins is diagnostic of chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Ongoing disease may occur despite cessation if fibrosis is established. (Source: TSANZ ILD Guidelines; ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT HP Guidelines)

options.text: Asbestosis from occupational farming exposure history
options.rationale: Asbestosis is caused by asbestos fibre exposure, not farming. It produces basal-predominant fibrosis similar to IPF. Farming does not typically involve asbestos exposure. The upper and mid-zone distribution with mosaic attenuation and positive avian precipitins are inconsistent with asbestosis. (Source: TSANZ)

explanation.summary: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis results from prolonged inhalation of organic antigens (avian proteins, moulds, chemicals). HRCT shows upper and mid-zone predominant ground-glass opacification, mosaic attenuation (reflecting small airways disease), and in chronic cases, fibrotic changes. IPF shows basal-predominant UIP pattern with honeycombing. Distinguishing chronic HP from IPF has critical management implications: HP may respond to antigen avoidance and immunosuppression, while IPF is treated with antifibrotics (pirfenidone or nintedanib). Multidisciplinary discussion involving radiology, pathology, and respiratory medicine is recommended.
key_points: Chronic HP: upper/mid-zone ground-glass, mosaic attenuation, exposure history
IPF: basal-predominant honeycombing, no identifiable cause
Serum precipitins support HP but can be positive without clinical disease
Multidisciplinary discussion is essential for ILD classification
explanation.pearls: Mosaic attenuation on HRCT is the radiological hallmark that distinguishes chronic HP from IPF, as it reflects small airways obstruction (air trapping) from bronchiolitis, which is a pathological feature of HP but not of UIP/IPF.

subtopic_detail: Chronic hepatitis B - treatment indication assessment
stem: A 38-year-old man born in Vietnam is found to be hepatitis B surface antigen positive on routine screening. He is asymptomatic. He was diagnosed with chronic hepatitis B 5 years ago in Vietnam but never commenced treatment. He has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications.

data_table.Investigations: HBsAg positive. HBeAg negative. Anti-HBe positive. HBV DNA 28,000 IU/mL. ALT 78 U/L (<40) - persistently elevated on 3 occasions over 6 months. AST 62 U/L. Platelet count 168 x10^9/L. FibroScan: 8.2 kPa (F2 fibrosis). AFP 4.2 mcg/L (<10). Hepatitis D antibody negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Monitor ALT and HBV DNA every 6 months without treatment
options.rationale: This patient meets treatment criteria: HBeAg-negative with persistently elevated ALT (>40 U/L on multiple occasions), HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL, and evidence of significant fibrosis (F2 on FibroScan). Monitoring alone risks disease progression to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. (Source: GESA CHB Guidelines; ASHM B Positive)

options.text: Pegylated interferon alfa-2a for 48 weeks as first-line
options.rationale: While pegylated interferon is an option for chronic hepatitis B, it is generally preferred in HBeAg-positive patients where seroconversion is the goal, younger patients, and those with higher ALT. In HBeAg-negative disease with significant fibrosis, long-term nucleos(t)ide analogue therapy is preferred due to its better tolerability and the typically indefinite treatment requirement. (Source: GESA; ASHM B Positive)

options.text: Lamivudine 100 mg daily as initial antiviral therapy
options.rationale: Lamivudine has a high barrier to resistance development (up to 70% at 5 years) and is no longer recommended as first-line monotherapy for chronic hepatitis B. Entecavir and tenofovir have high genetic barriers to resistance and are the preferred first-line agents per Australian guidelines. (Source: GESA; ASHM B Positive; AMH)

options.text: Liver biopsy for histological grading before treatment
options.rationale: FibroScan (transient elastography) has largely replaced liver biopsy for fibrosis staging in chronic hepatitis B. This patient already has a FibroScan showing F2 fibrosis, which, combined with persistently elevated ALT and HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL, is sufficient to establish the treatment indication. Liver biopsy is not required before initiating therapy. (Source: GESA)

options.text: Entecavir 0.5 mg daily as long-term antiviral therapy
options.rationale: Entecavir is a first-line nucleoside analogue with a high genetic barrier to resistance (<1.2% at 6 years in treatment-naive patients). This patient meets treatment criteria: HBeAg-negative disease with persistently elevated ALT, HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL, and F2 fibrosis on FibroScan. Tenofovir disoproxil or tenofovir alafenamide are equally appropriate alternatives. Treatment is typically indefinite in HBeAg-negative disease. (Source: GESA CHB Guidelines; ASHM B Positive; AMH)

explanation.summary: Treatment of chronic hepatitis B is indicated when there is evidence of active viral replication (HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL), liver inflammation (persistently elevated ALT), and/or significant fibrosis. First-line nucleos(t)ide analogues in Australia are entecavir, tenofovir disoproxil, and tenofovir alafenamide, which have high genetic barriers to resistance. Lamivudine and adefovir are no longer recommended as first-line agents due to resistance. HBeAg-negative patients typically require indefinite therapy as relapse rates after cessation are high. Regular HCC surveillance with 6-monthly ultrasound and AFP is mandatory.
key_points: Treatment criteria: HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL + elevated ALT + fibrosis evidence
First-line agents: entecavir, tenofovir disoproxil, or tenofovir alafenamide
Lamivudine has unacceptable resistance rates and is not first-line
HBeAg-negative disease usually requires indefinite antiviral therapy
explanation.pearls: Entecavir resistance rates are <1.2% at 6 years in treatment-naive patients but rise to 51% at 5 years in lamivudine-experienced patients due to cross-resistance, making treatment history a critical factor in antiviral selection for chronic hepatitis B.

subtopic_detail: Severe alcohol-related hepatitis - Maddrey discriminant function and prednisolone
stem: A 48-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol consumption (>80 g/day for 20 years) presents with 2 weeks of progressive jaundice, right upper quadrant tenderness, and fever. He last consumed alcohol 3 days ago. He has no evidence of GI bleeding or encephalopathy. An abdominal ultrasound shows hepatomegaly with no biliary obstruction.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, Temp 38.2 degrees C.
data_table.Investigations: Bilirubin 182 micromol/L (<20). ALT 88 U/L (<40). AST 196 U/L (<35). ALP 168 U/L (30-110). INR 2.1. Albumin 26 g/L (35-50). WCC 14.6 x10^9/L. Platelet 98 x10^9/L. Creatinine 92 micromol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Prednisolone 40 mg daily for 28 days with Lille assessment
options.rationale: This patient has severe alcohol-related hepatitis based on the Maddrey discriminant function. DF = 4.6 x (patient INR - control INR) + bilirubin in micromol/L / 17.1. His DF exceeds 32, indicating severe disease with high short-term mortality. Prednisolone 40 mg daily for 28 days is recommended, with Lille score assessment at day 7 to determine treatment response and guide continuation. (Source: GESA; eTG Gastro; EASL Guidelines)

options.text: N-acetylcysteine infusion alone without corticosteroids
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine has shown benefit as adjunctive therapy with prednisolone in severe alcoholic hepatitis (improved 1-month survival in the AASLD trial) but is not recommended as sole therapy. It does not replace corticosteroids for severe disease. It is used as monotherapy only when corticosteroids are contraindicated. (Source: GESA; EASL)

options.text: Pentoxifylline 400 mg three times daily for 28 days
options.rationale: Pentoxifylline was previously considered an alternative to prednisolone, particularly in the setting of contraindications. However, the STOPAH trial showed no survival benefit with pentoxifylline, and it is no longer recommended for severe alcoholic hepatitis by current guidelines. (Source: GESA; STOPAH Trial; EASL)

options.text: Ursodeoxycholic acid 15 mg/kg daily for cholestasis
options.rationale: Ursodeoxycholic acid is used for primary biliary cholangitis and gallstone dissolution but has no proven benefit in alcoholic hepatitis. The jaundice here is from hepatocellular injury, not bile duct obstruction. Corticosteroids are the appropriate intervention for severe alcoholic hepatitis. (Source: GESA; eTG Gastro)

options.text: Supportive care only with nutritional supplementation
options.rationale: While nutritional support is essential (patients require 35-40 kcal/kg/day and 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day protein), supportive care alone is inadequate for severe alcoholic hepatitis with DF >=32, which carries a 28-day mortality of 30-50% without corticosteroid treatment. (Source: GESA; EASL)

explanation.summary: Severe alcohol-related hepatitis is defined by a Maddrey discriminant function >=32 or MELD score >=21, both indicating high 28-day mortality (30-50% without treatment). Prednisolone 40 mg daily for 28 days is the standard of care, with the Lille score at day 7 assessing treatment response: a Lille score >0.45 indicates non-response and corticosteroids should be ceased. Contraindications to prednisolone include active GI bleeding, uncontrolled infection, and hepatorenal syndrome. Nutritional support and alcohol cessation counselling are essential adjuncts. Early liver transplant referral is considered for non-responders in select centres.
key_points: Maddrey DF >=32 or MELD >=21 defines severe alcoholic hepatitis requiring treatment
Prednisolone 40 mg daily for 28 days is the standard treatment
Lille score at day 7 guides continuation - score >0.45 indicates non-response
Pentoxifylline no longer recommended following the STOPAH trial
explanation.pearls: The Maddrey discriminant function is calculated as 4.6 x (patient PT in seconds - control PT in seconds) + serum bilirubin in mg/dL, but in Australian practice using micromol/L and INR, the formula is modified: DF = 4.6 x (patient INR - 1.0) x 12 + bilirubin (micromol/L) / 17.1.

subtopic_detail: Pituitary apoplexy - recognition and emergency management
stem: A 50-year-old man with a known 18 mm pituitary macroadenoma (non-functioning) under surveillance presents to the emergency department with sudden severe thunderclap headache, vomiting, and acute visual deterioration over 4 hours. He reports he can no longer see objects in his right visual field with either eye. He was commenced on anticoagulation with apixaban 3 weeks ago for a new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 52 bpm, BP 88/54 mmHg, Temp 36.2 degrees C.
data_table.Examination: GCS 14 (E3V5M6). Right homonymous hemianopia on confrontation. Left partial third nerve palsy with ptosis. Meningism present.
data_table.Investigations: CT head: haemorrhagic pituitary mass with suprasellar extension. Sodium 128 mmol/L. Cortisol 42 nmol/L (morning reference 150-600). TSH 0.3 mU/L. Free T4 8.2 pmol/L (10-20).

lead_in: What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.text: Urgent MRI pituitary before any treatment decision
options.rationale: While MRI provides superior imaging of pituitary apoplexy, waiting for MRI delays critical treatment. CT has already confirmed a haemorrhagic pituitary mass. The immediate priority is haemodynamic stabilisation with IV hydrocortisone, as cortisol deficiency causes life-threatening adrenal crisis. (Source: Pituitary Society Guidelines; eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat and fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Pituitary apoplexy causes acute hypopituitarism. Adrenocorticotropic hormone deficiency results in cortisol deficiency with haemodynamic collapse. The cortisol of 42 nmol/L confirms severe deficiency. IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat must be given immediately, followed by 50-100 mg every 6-8 hours, before any further investigations or neurosurgical intervention. IV fluid resuscitation addresses the hypotension. (Source: Pituitary Society Guidelines; eTG Endocrinology; Endocrine Society)

options.text: Emergency neurosurgical decompression within 6 hours
options.rationale: Neurosurgical decompression may be indicated for progressive visual deterioration or reduced consciousness, but it is not the MOST CRITICAL immediate intervention. Without cortisol replacement, the patient is at risk of cardiovascular collapse during induction of anaesthesia. Hydrocortisone must be administered before any surgical planning. (Source: Pituitary Society Guidelines)

options.text: Levothyroxine 100 mcg daily for secondary hypothyroidism
options.rationale: While the low free T4 and low-normal TSH indicate secondary hypothyroidism, thyroid replacement must NEVER be commenced before cortisol replacement in panhypopituitarism. Thyroxine increases cortisol metabolism and can precipitate adrenal crisis in a cortisol-deficient patient. Hydrocortisone must be given first. (Source: eTG Endocrinology; AMH)

options.text: Reversal of apixaban with andexanet alfa infusion
options.rationale: While apixaban may have contributed to the haemorrhagic event, andexanet alfa is reserved for life-threatening or uncontrolled bleeding. The pituitary haemorrhage is typically self-contained and the immediate life threat is adrenal crisis from cortisol deficiency. Anticoagulant reversal is a secondary consideration after cortisol replacement and haemodynamic stabilisation. (Source: THANZ)

explanation.summary: Pituitary apoplexy is a clinical syndrome resulting from haemorrhage or infarction of the pituitary gland, usually into a pre-existing adenoma. It presents with sudden headache, visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia from chiasmal compression), ophthalmoplegia (cranial nerves III, IV, VI), and hypopituitarism. The most critical immediate management is IV hydrocortisone, as acute ACTH deficiency causes adrenal crisis with cardiovascular collapse. Thyroxine replacement must be delayed until cortisol is replete. Neurosurgical decompression is indicated for progressive visual deterioration or reduced consciousness.
key_points: IV hydrocortisone is the most critical immediate intervention in pituitary apoplexy
Never start thyroxine before cortisol replacement in panhypopituitarism
Cortisol level <100 nmol/L in acute illness indicates severe deficiency
Neurosurgical decompression is indicated for progressive visual loss or reduced GCS
explanation.pearls: The rule of cortisol-before-thyroxine in panhypopituitarism exists because thyroxine increases hepatic cortisol metabolism via CYP3A4 induction, which in a cortisol-deficient patient can precipitate fatal adrenal crisis within days of starting levothyroxine.

subtopic_detail: Beta-thalassaemia trait vs iron deficiency - discrimination
stem: A 28-year-old woman of Mediterranean ancestry presents for a routine antenatal visit at 12 weeks gestation. Her GP noted microcytic anaemia on her initial bloods. She has no symptoms of anaemia and reports a normal diet. She has had mild microcytic anaemia documented on previous blood tests but was never investigated further.

data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L (115-160). MCV 64 fL (80-100). MCH 22 pg (27-32). RCC 5.8 x10^12/L (3.8-5.2). RDW 13.5% (<14.5). Ferritin 68 mcg/L (20-200). Iron 14 micromol/L (10-30). Transferrin saturation 28% (15-45).

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of her microcytic anaemia?

options.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from dietary insufficiency
options.rationale: Iron studies are normal (ferritin 68, transferrin saturation 28%), which effectively excludes iron deficiency as the cause. In IDA, ferritin would be low (<20), iron low, and transferrin saturation low (<16%). The normal iron status points to a non-iron-related cause of microcytosis. (Source: eTG Haematology; THANZ)

options.text: Anaemia of chronic disease from pregnancy physiology
options.rationale: Anaemia of chronic disease typically shows normal or elevated ferritin with low iron and low transferrin saturation. At 12 weeks gestation, physiological haemodilution has not yet significantly affected haemoglobin. Additionally, ACD does not typically cause the degree of microcytosis seen here (MCV 64). (Source: eTG Haematology)

options.text: Beta-thalassaemia trait with characteristic red cell indices
options.rationale: The combination of microcytosis (MCV 64) disproportionate to the degree of anaemia (Hb 108), elevated red cell count (5.8 - compensatory erythrocytosis), normal RDW (<14.5), normal iron studies, and Mediterranean ancestry is characteristic of beta-thalassaemia trait. The Mentzer index (MCV/RCC = 64/5.8 = 11) is below 13, supporting thalassaemia over iron deficiency. Haemoglobin electrophoresis will confirm elevated HbA2. (Source: eTG Haematology; THANZ; RANZCOG)

options.text: Sideroblastic anaemia from pyridoxine deficiency
options.rationale: Sideroblastic anaemia is rare and typically presents with elevated iron stores (high ferritin, high transferrin saturation) and ring sideroblasts on bone marrow biopsy. This patient has normal iron studies and a pattern more consistent with a haemoglobinopathy. Pyridoxine deficiency sufficient to cause sideroblastic anaemia is uncommon in a well-nourished patient. (Source: eTG Haematology)

options.text: Alpha-thalassaemia with two-gene deletion carrier state
options.rationale: While alpha-thalassaemia trait (two-gene deletion) can also cause mild microcytic anaemia with normal iron studies, it is more prevalent in South-East Asian and African populations. In a patient of Mediterranean ancestry, beta-thalassaemia trait is far more common. Alpha-thalassaemia is confirmed by genetic testing, not haemoglobin electrophoresis. (Source: THANZ)

explanation.summary: Beta-thalassaemia trait is characterised by mild microcytic anaemia (Hb usually >90 g/L) with disproportionately low MCV, elevated red cell count, and normal iron studies. The Mentzer index (MCV divided by RCC) below 13 favours thalassaemia over iron deficiency. Confirmation is by haemoglobin electrophoresis showing elevated HbA2 (>3.5%). In pregnancy, partner testing is essential to assess the risk of beta-thalassaemia major in offspring. Pre-conception or early antenatal screening is recommended for high-risk ethnic groups.
key_points: Mentzer index (MCV/RCC) <13 favours thalassaemia; >13 favours iron deficiency
Normal iron studies exclude iron deficiency as the cause of microcytosis
Normal RDW with microcytosis suggests thalassaemia (IDA has elevated RDW)
Haemoglobin electrophoresis showing HbA2 >3.5% confirms beta-thalassaemia trait
explanation.pearls: In pregnancy, if both parents carry beta-thalassaemia trait, there is a 25% chance of beta-thalassaemia major in each offspring, making partner testing and genetic counselling before 16 weeks gestation essential per RANZCOG guidelines to allow time for prenatal diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: Post-cardiac arrest - targeted temperature management and neuroprognostication
stem: A 58-year-old man achieves return of spontaneous circulation after 22 minutes of CPR for witnessed ventricular fibrillation in a shopping centre. He remains comatose (GCS 3T) on arrival to the emergency department. He is intubated and ventilated. His initial ECG post-ROSC shows sinus tachycardia with no ST elevation. A CT brain is unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108 bpm, BP 92/58 mmHg (on noradrenaline 0.1 mcg/kg/min), SpO2 96% on FiO2 0.5.
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 8.4 mmol/L. pH 7.18. Potassium 3.8 mmol/L. Glucose 12.4 mmol/L. Troponin 420 ng/L. CT brain: no acute intracranial pathology.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate targeted temperature management strategy?

options.text: Therapeutic hypothermia at 33 degrees C for 24 hours
options.rationale: The TTM2 trial (2021) demonstrated no benefit of 33 degrees C over targeted normothermia (<=37.5 degrees C) for neurological outcome after cardiac arrest. Current ANZCOR and ILCOR guidelines recommend preventing fever (>37.7 degrees C) rather than active cooling to 33 degrees C. Active hypothermia at 33 degrees C is no longer the standard recommendation. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.9; TTM2 Trial; ILCOR 2022)

options.text: Cool to 36 degrees C for 24 hours per TTM1 protocol
options.rationale: The TTM1 trial showed equivalent outcomes at 33 degrees C vs 36 degrees C, but the subsequent TTM2 trial showed that even 33 degrees C provides no benefit over normothermia with fever prevention. Current best practice is targeted normothermia with active fever prevention rather than targeting a specific hypothermic temperature. (Source: ANZCOR; TTM2 Trial)

options.text: No active temperature management needed in this case
options.rationale: While active hypothermia is no longer routinely recommended, active fever prevention is essential. Post-cardiac arrest fever (>37.7 degrees C) is associated with worse neurological outcomes. Targeted normothermia at <=37.5 degrees C for at least 72 hours using active temperature control devices is the current standard. (Source: ANZCOR; ILCOR 2022)

options.text: Targeted normothermia at 37.5 degrees C with active fever prevention
options.rationale: Following the TTM2 trial, current ANZCOR and ILCOR guidelines recommend targeted normothermia (<=37.5 degrees C) with active fever prevention for at least 72 hours after ROSC. This involves continuous core temperature monitoring and active cooling measures if temperature exceeds 37.7 degrees C. Neuroprognostication should not occur before 72 hours post-ROSC. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 11.9; ILCOR 2022; ANZICS)

options.text: Rapid cooling to 32 degrees C within 4 hours of ROSC
options.rationale: Rapid cooling to 32 degrees C is an aggressive hypothermia protocol that is not supported by current evidence. The TTM2 trial and HYPERION trial data do not support targeting temperatures below 33 degrees C. Additionally, rapid cooling can cause haemodynamic instability and arrhythmias. Targeted normothermia is the current recommendation. (Source: ANZCOR; TTM2 Trial)

explanation.summary: Post-cardiac arrest care follows a structured protocol: targeted temperature management, haemodynamic optimisation, coronary angiography if STEMI is suspected, seizure management, and delayed neuroprognostication. The TTM2 trial (2021) changed practice by showing no benefit of 33 degrees C hypothermia over normothermia with fever prevention. Current ANZCOR guidelines recommend targeted normothermia (<=37.5 degrees C) with active fever prevention for at least 72 hours. Neuroprognostication using multimodal assessment should not occur before 72 hours post-ROSC to avoid premature withdrawal of treatment.
key_points: TTM2 trial: no benefit of 33 degrees C vs normothermia with fever prevention
Current recommendation: targeted normothermia <=37.5 degrees C for >=72 hours
Active fever prevention is essential - fever >37.7 degrees C worsens outcomes
Neuroprognostication should not occur before 72 hours post-ROSC
explanation.pearls: Multimodal neuroprognostication at >=72 hours combines bilateral absent pupillary and corneal reflexes, absent N20 on somatosensory evoked potentials, highly malignant EEG, serum NSE >60 mcg/L at 48-72h, and diffuse cerebral oedema on CT/MRI, with at least two concordant poor prognostic indicators required before considering treatment withdrawal per ILCOR guidance.

subtopic_detail: Smoking cessation with clozapine - CYP1A2 interaction management
stem: A 42-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia stabilised on clozapine 400 mg daily wants to quit smoking. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day. His most recent clozapine trough level is 0.42 mg/L (therapeutic range 0.35-0.60 mg/L). He has no cardiac risk factors, no seizure history, and his metabolic monitoring is stable.

data_table.Investigations: Clozapine trough level 0.42 mg/L (0.35-0.60). FBC: WCC 6.8 x10^9/L, neutrophils 3.4 x10^9/L. Fasting glucose 5.8 mmol/L. HbA1c 5.6%. Fasting lipids within normal limits. ECG: QTc 418 ms.

lead_in: What is the most important pharmacological consideration when supporting his smoking cessation?

options.text: Varenicline is contraindicated due to psychiatric illness
options.rationale: The EAGLES trial demonstrated that varenicline does not increase neuropsychiatric adverse events in patients with stable psychiatric illness compared to placebo. Varenicline is effective and generally safe in schizophrenia. The critical consideration in this patient is the clozapine-smoking interaction. (Source: RANZCP CPG; AMH; EAGLES Trial)

options.text: Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold with clozapine
options.rationale: While bupropion does lower the seizure threshold and clozapine also carries dose-dependent seizure risk, this is a secondary consideration. The most critical pharmacological issue is that smoking cessation removes CYP1A2 induction, causing clozapine levels to rise by 40-70%, which can produce toxicity including seizures, sedation, and myocarditis. (Source: AMH; eTG Psychotropic)

options.text: Smoking cessation raises clozapine levels via CYP1A2 changes
options.rationale: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in cigarette smoke induce CYP1A2, which metabolises clozapine. Smoking cessation removes this induction, causing clozapine plasma levels to increase by 40-70% within days. This can result in toxicity (excessive sedation, seizures, myocarditis). The clozapine dose must be proactively reduced by approximately 30-40% at the time of cessation, with frequent level monitoring. (Source: AMH; RANZCP CPG; eTG Psychotropic)

options.text: NRT patches are contraindicated with antipsychotic therapy
options.rationale: NRT (patches, lozenges, gum) is safe with antipsychotic therapy and does not interact with clozapine metabolism. NRT delivers nicotine without the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons that cause CYP1A2 induction, meaning NRT itself does not affect clozapine levels. The clozapine dose adjustment is needed due to cessation of smoking, not due to NRT use. (Source: AMH; RANZCP)

options.text: Clozapine dose should be increased to prevent relapse
options.rationale: Increasing the clozapine dose would be dangerous. The opposite adjustment is required: clozapine dose should be reduced by approximately 30-40% when smoking ceases, because removal of CYP1A2 induction by cigarette smoke causes clozapine levels to rise significantly. Dose increases would compound this effect and risk serious toxicity. (Source: AMH; RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Cigarette smoke (specifically polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, not nicotine) induces CYP1A2, which is the primary enzyme metabolising clozapine and olanzapine. When a patient on clozapine stops smoking, CYP1A2 activity decreases over 3-7 days, causing clozapine plasma levels to rise by 40-70%. This can produce toxicity including excessive sedation, sialorrhoea, seizures, and potentially myocarditis. The clozapine dose should be proactively reduced by 30-40% at the time of smoking cessation, with clozapine levels monitored at baseline, then weekly for 4 weeks. NRT does not affect CYP1A2 induction as it delivers nicotine without combustion products.
key_points: Smoking cessation raises clozapine levels by 40-70% via loss of CYP1A2 induction
Clozapine dose should be reduced by 30-40% at cessation
It is the smoke (PAHs), not nicotine, that induces CYP1A2
NRT does not affect clozapine metabolism
explanation.pearls: The CYP1A2 de-induction after smoking cessation begins within 24 hours and is 90% complete by day 7, meaning clozapine toxicity can manifest as early as 2-3 days after the last cigarette, necessitating immediate dose reduction rather than waiting for level results.

subtopic_detail: GHB withdrawal - recognition and benzodiazepine management
stem: A 29-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe agitation, tremor, diaphoresis, and tachycardia. His housemate reports he uses GHB (gamma-hydroxybutyrate) daily - approximately 5-10 mL every 2-3 hours including overnight - and his last dose was 8 hours ago when he ran out. He has no history of alcohol use disorder. He has had one previous episode of GHB withdrawal requiring ICU admission 6 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128 bpm, BP 168/98 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 38.4 degrees C.
data_table.Examination: Agitated, diaphoretic, coarse tremor in all limbs. Dilated pupils. No focal neurological deficit. No signs of head injury.
data_table.Investigations: BSL 6.2 mmol/L. UEC normal. LFTs normal. Blood alcohol 0. Urine drug screen: negative for amphetamines and opioids.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?

options.text: Diazepam 20 mg IV with repeated doses titrated to effect
options.rationale: GHB withdrawal requires aggressive benzodiazepine dosing, often exceeding doses used in alcohol withdrawal. Diazepam is preferred due to its rapid onset and long half-life. Doses of 20-40 mg IV may be needed initially, with frequent reassessment and repeated dosing. GHB acts on GABA-B and GHB receptors, and withdrawal produces a hyperadrenergic state that is often refractory to standard benzodiazepine doses. ICU admission is frequently required. (Source: eTG Toxicology; NSW Health GHB Guidelines; RANZCP)

options.text: Chlordiazepoxide loading protocol as per alcohol withdrawal
options.rationale: Chlordiazepoxide is used for alcohol withdrawal but GHB withdrawal is pharmacologically distinct and often more severe. GHB withdrawal has a faster onset (1-6 hours vs 12-48 hours for alcohol), can be refractory to standard benzodiazepine doses, and may require much higher total doses. IV diazepam with titration to effect is preferred for the rapid symptom control needed. (Source: eTG Toxicology; NSW Health)

options.text: Baclofen 10 mg three times daily for GABA-B agonism
options.rationale: While baclofen (a GABA-B agonist) has theoretical rationale in GHB withdrawal and case reports support its use, it has no established dosing protocol and limited evidence base. It does not adequately treat the acute severe withdrawal syndrome and cannot be given IV. Benzodiazepines remain first-line for acute GHB withdrawal management. (Source: eTG Toxicology)

options.text: Haloperidol 5 mg IM for acute agitation management
options.rationale: Antipsychotics do not treat the underlying GABA-ergic withdrawal and may lower the seizure threshold. Haloperidol can also cause QT prolongation and extrapyramidal effects. The agitation in GHB withdrawal requires GABAergic treatment (benzodiazepines), not antidopaminergic agents. (Source: eTG Toxicology; NSW Health)

options.text: Phenobarbitone 130 mg IV as adjunctive sedative agent
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone may be used as a second-line agent for GHB withdrawal refractory to high-dose benzodiazepines, but it is not the most appropriate initial treatment. First-line management is aggressive IV benzodiazepine titration. Phenobarbitone carries risks of respiratory depression and accumulation, and is considered only after benzodiazepine failure. (Source: eTG Toxicology)

explanation.summary: GHB (gamma-hydroxybutyrate) withdrawal is a medical emergency that develops within 1-6 hours of the last dose in dependent users, progresses rapidly, and can be life-threatening with delirium, seizures, and autonomic instability. It differs from alcohol withdrawal in its rapid onset, potential severity, and frequent refractoriness to standard benzodiazepine doses. Management requires aggressive IV benzodiazepine titration, often requiring total doses of 100-500 mg diazepam equivalent in the first 24 hours. ICU admission is frequently necessary. Phenobarbitone or propofol may be needed for refractory cases.
key_points: GHB withdrawal onset is rapid (1-6 hours) compared to alcohol (12-48 hours)
High-dose IV benzodiazepines are first-line, often far exceeding alcohol withdrawal doses
ICU admission is frequently required for monitoring and escalation
Antipsychotics do not treat the underlying withdrawal and may worsen outcomes
explanation.pearls: GHB-dependent users who dose every 2-3 hours around the clock can develop withdrawal within 1 hour of a missed dose due to GHB's short half-life (30-60 minutes), which is why GHB withdrawal can present more acutely than alcohol withdrawal and requires immediate aggressive benzodiazepine treatment.

subtopic_detail: Cannabis-induced psychosis vs primary psychotic disorder - discrimination
stem: A 21-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after threatening passersby, believing they were conspiring against him. He has been using cannabis daily for 4 years, with escalating use to multiple cones per day over the past 6 months. His urine drug screen is positive for THC only. He reports auditory hallucinations commanding him to protect himself. There is no family history of psychotic disorders. He was functioning well at university until 3 months ago when his cannabis use increased significantly.

data_table.Examination: Agitated, suspicious, poor eye contact. Persecutory delusions - believes strangers are government agents. Third-person auditory hallucinations. Thought form is linear. Oriented to time, place, and person. No evidence of formal thought disorder.
data_table.Investigations: UDS: THC positive, all others negative. BSL 5.4 mmol/L. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs - all normal.

lead_in: Which clinical feature most strongly favours substance-induced psychotic disorder over a primary psychotic disorder in this presentation?

options.text: The presence of persecutory delusions and auditory command hallucinations
options.rationale: Both persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations occur in both substance-induced psychosis and primary psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia. Their presence alone does not discriminate between the two diagnoses. The phenomenology of psychosis is often indistinguishable; temporal relationship to substance use is the key discriminator. (Source: RANZCP CPG; DSM-5)

options.text: Symptom resolution within one month of confirmed cannabis abstinence
options.rationale: The key discriminating feature between substance-induced psychotic disorder and primary psychotic disorder is the temporal relationship to substance use. Per DSM-5, if psychotic symptoms resolve within approximately one month of sustained abstinence, the diagnosis is substance-induced. If symptoms persist beyond one month of confirmed abstinence, the diagnosis should be reconsidered as a primary psychotic disorder. Cannabis-induced psychosis typically resolves within 1-4 weeks of cessation. (Source: RANZCP CPG; DSM-5; eTG Psychotropic)

options.text: The absence of formal thought disorder in this presentation
options.rationale: Formal thought disorder (tangentiality, loose associations, word salad) is associated with schizophrenia but can also be absent in early presentations. Its absence does not reliably distinguish substance-induced from primary psychosis. Many patients with first-episode schizophrenia present without formal thought disorder. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: The negative family history for any psychotic disorders
options.rationale: While family history of psychotic disorders increases the pre-test probability of primary psychosis, its absence does not exclude schizophrenia. Approximately 80% of schizophrenia cases have no first-degree relative with the condition. Family history is a risk modifier but not a discriminating feature for the index presentation. (Source: RANZCP CPG)

options.text: The good premorbid functioning until cannabis use escalated
options.rationale: Good premorbid functioning is common in both substance-induced psychosis and some presentations of first-episode schizophrenia. While poor premorbid functioning increases suspicion for a primary psychotic disorder, good premorbid function does not exclude it. The temporal relationship to substance use remains the most reliable discriminator. (Source: RANZCP CPG; DSM-5)

explanation.summary: Distinguishing cannabis-induced psychotic disorder from a primary psychotic disorder is a common clinical challenge. The DSM-5 specifies that substance-induced psychotic disorder is diagnosed when symptoms are temporally related to substance use and resolve within approximately one month of abstinence. Cannabis-induced psychosis typically resolves within 1-4 weeks of cessation. However, heavy cannabis use doubles the lifetime risk of developing schizophrenia, and approximately 25-50% of patients initially diagnosed with cannabis-induced psychosis will be reclassified as having a primary psychotic disorder within 3 years. Close follow-up with repeat assessment during abstinence is essential.
key_points: Symptom resolution within 1 month of confirmed abstinence favours substance-induced diagnosis
Persistence beyond 1 month favours reclassification to primary psychotic disorder
25-50% of cannabis-induced psychosis converts to primary psychotic disorder within 3 years
Heavy cannabis use doubles lifetime risk of developing schizophrenia
explanation.pearls: High-potency cannabis (>10% THC content) carries a 5-fold increased risk of psychosis compared to never-use, and daily use of high-potency cannabis is the strongest modifiable risk factor for first-episode psychosis in young adults, accounting for approximately 20% of new psychosis cases in cities with high availability.
